- **Hemodynamic Models**: Pressure–volume relations in circulatory system models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlight — Why not just use Taylor Series?**
> Taylor series methods require computing higher-order derivatives ($\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}, \frac{d^3y}{dx^3}, \dots$) of the function $f(x,y)$, which becomes prohibitively expensive and error-prone for complex biomedical models. RK methods achieve the *same order of accuracy* by only requiring evaluations of $f(x,y)$ itself at multiple points — a massive practical advantage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Runge–Kutta Works

The general Runge–Kutta method advances the solution from $x_n$ to $x_{n+1} = x_n + h$ using a formula of the form:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \sum_{i=1}^{s} c_i \, k_i$$

where each *stage* (intermediate slope estimate) is computed as:

$$k_i = f\!\left(x_n + h \cdot a_i, \ y_n + h \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} b_{ij} \, k_j \right), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, s$$

The constants $a_i, b_{ij}, c_i$ are chosen so that the Taylor series expansion of $y_{n+1}$ matches the true Taylor series of the solution up to a desired order. This matching process (called **order conditions**) determines the coefficients uniquely for low-order methods.

### 2.2 Logic Steps Behind an Order-$p$ RK Method

1. **Express the increment** $\Delta y = y_{n+1} - y_n$ as a linear combination of $s$ stage evaluations $k_1, k_2, \dots, k_s$.
2. **Expand $y_{n+1}$ in Taylor series** about $(x_n, y_n)$ up to order $h^p$.
3. **Expand each stage** $k_i = f(x_n + \alpha_i h, y_n + \beta_i h)$ using the bivariate Taylor series of $f(x,y)$.
4. **Substitute** and equate coefficients of like powers of $h$ on both sides.
5. **Solve the resulting nonlinear system** for the RK parameters $\{a_i, b_{ij}, c_i\}$.
6. The method is **order $p$** if the Taylor series matches exactly up to and including $h^p$ but mismatches at $h^{p+1}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects all RK formulas essential for KTU 2024 examination preparation. Note the use of $\vert \cdot \vert$ instead of $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Method | Order $p$ | Stage Count $s$ | Step Formula | Local Truncation Error (LTE) | Global Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Euler** | 1 | 1 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,k_1$, $\;k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h)$ |
| **RK2 (Heun's)** | 2 | 2 | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$ ; $k_2 = f(x_n+h, y_n+h k_1)$ ; $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}(k_1+k_2)$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| **RK2 (Midpoint)** | 2 | 2 | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$ ; $k_2 = f(x_n+\tfrac{h}{2}, y_n+\tfrac{h}{2} k_1)$ ; $y_{n+1} = y_n + h k_2$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| **RK2 (Ralston's)** | 2 | 2 | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$ ; $k_2 = f(x_n+\tfrac{3h}{4}, y_n+\tfrac{3h}{4} k_1)$ ; $y_{n+1} = y_n + h\!\left(\tfrac{1}{3} k_1 + \tfrac{2}{3} k_2\right)$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| **RK3 (Classical)** | 3 | 3 | See derivation below | $O(h^4)$ | $O(h^3)$ |
| **RK4 (Classical)** | 4 | 4 | See derivation below | $O(h^5)$ | $O(h^4)$ |

### 2.4 The Butcher Tableau (Standard Form)

A compact way to represent any RK method is the **Butcher Tableau**:

$$\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 & & & & \\
a_2 & b_{21} & & & \\
a_3 & b_{31} & b_{32} & & \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \\
a_s & b_{s1} & b_{s2} & \cdots & b_{s,s-1} \\ \hline
& c_1 & c_2 & \cdots & c_s
\end{array}$$

For the **classical RK4**, the tableau is:

$$\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 & & & & \\
\frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & & & \\
\frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{1}{2} & & \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & \\ \hline
& \frac{1}{6} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{6}
\end{array}$$

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of RK Methods

- **Stability**: RK4 has a much larger stability region than Euler, allowing larger step sizes $h$ for stable computation.
- **Self-Starting**: Unlike multi-step methods (Adams–Bashforth, etc.), RK methods are **single-step** and require only the initial value $y_0$ — no starter formula needed.
- **Ease of Step-Size Control**: Embedded RK pairs (e.g., **RK45 Dormand–Prince**, used in MATLAB's `ode45`) use two methods of different orders sharing the same stages to estimate local error and adapt $h$ automatically.
- **Biomedical Relevance**: When modeling stiff ODEs (e.g., fast neuronal dynamics), implicit RK methods (Radau, Lobatto) are used; the explicit RK4 is the workhorse for non-stiff biological systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Function** for explicit RK4: $R(z) = 1 + z + \frac{z^2}{2!} + \frac{z^3}{3!} + \frac{z^4}{4!}$, which is the first five terms of $e^z$. This is why RK4 is essentially *truncating* the exponential solution of the linear test equation $\dot y = \lambda y$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of RK2 (Heun's Method) — Detailed

**Goal:** Construct a 2-stage RK method of the form
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + a\,k_1 + b\,k_2$$
with
$$k_1 = h\,f(x_n, y_n), \quad k_2 = h\,f(x_n + \alpha h, \, y_n + \beta k_1)$$

**Step 1 — Taylor Expand the True Solution.** The exact solution at $x_{n+1}$ is:

$$y(x_{n+1}) = y(x_n) + h y'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2} y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{6} y'''(x_n) + O(h^4)$$

Using $y' = f(x,y)$ and differentiating:

$$y'' = f_x + f_y y' = f_x + f_y f$$
$$y''' = f_{xx} + 2 f_{xy} f + f_{yy} f^2 + f_y f_x + f_y^2 f$$

**Step 2 — Expand $k_2$ Using Bivariate Taylor Series.**

$$k_2 = h\left[f(x_n, y_n) + \alpha h f_x(x_n, y_n) + \beta k_1 f_y(x_n, y_n) + O(h^2)\right]$$

Substituting $k_1 = h f$:

$$k_2 = h f + \alpha h^2 f_x + \beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Step 3 — Form the Linear Combination.**

$$a k_1 + b k_2 = a h f + b\left[h f + \alpha h^2 f_x + \beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)\right]$$

$$= (a+b) h f + b \alpha h^2 f_x + b \beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the True Increment.** The exact increment is:

$$\Delta y = h f + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_x + f f_y) + O(h^3)$$

Matching coefficients of $h f, h^2 f_x, h^2 f f_y$:

| Coefficient | Equation | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| $h f$ | $a + b = 1$ | — |
| $h^2 f_x$ | $b \alpha = \frac{1}{2}$ | — |
| $h^2 f f_y$ | $b \beta = \frac{1}{2}$ | — |

**Step 5 — Solve the System.** Choosing $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$ (most common — **Heun's method**):

$$b = \frac{1}{2}, \quad a = 1 - b = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \alpha = 1, \quad \beta = 1$$

**Final RK2 (Heun's) Formulas:**

$$\boxed{\,k_1 = f(x_n, y_n),\quad k_2 = f(x_n + h,\, y_n + h k_1),\quad y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The local truncation error is $LTE = \frac{h^3}{6}\left[\tfrac{1}{2}f_{xx} + f_{xy} f + \tfrac{1}{2}f_{yy} f^2 + f_y(f_x + f f_y)\right]$, confirming the method is **order 2**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of RK4 (Classical Fourth-Order Runge–Kutta) — Detailed

**General 4-Stage Form:**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + a_1 k_1 + a_2 k_2 + a_3 k_3 + a_4 k_4$$

$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$$
$$k_2 = h f(x_n + p_1 h, \, y_n + q_{11} k_1)$$
$$k_3 = h f(x_n + p_2 h, \, y_n + q_{21} k_1 + q_{22} k_2)$$
$$k_4 = h f(x_n + p_3 h, \, y_n + q_{31} k_1 + q_{32} k_2 + q_{33} k_3)$$

This method has **13 unknown parameters** and we need to satisfy **order conditions** up to $h^4$.

**Step 1 — True Taylor Series (up to $h^4$):**

$$y(x_{n+1}) - y(x_n) = h f + \frac{h^2}{2} Df + \frac{h^3}{6}\left[D^2 f + f_y Df\right] + \frac{h^4}{24}\left[D^3 f + f_y D^2 f + Df \cdot Df_y\right] + O(h^5)$$

where $D = \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + f \frac{\partial}{\partial y}$ is the **total derivative operator** along the solution.

For compactness, denote $f = f(x_n, y_n)$ and the partial derivatives are evaluated at $(x_n, y_n)$.

**Step 2 — Expand Each Stage.** After bivariate Taylor expansion of $f$ around $(x_n, y_n)$ and collection of like powers of $h$, we obtain expressions for $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$.

**Step 3 — Form $a_1 k_1 + a_2 k_2 + a_3 k_3 + a_4 k_4$ and Match.** This produces a system of nonlinear algebraic equations in the 13 unknowns.

**Step 4 — Resulting System and Classical Solution.** The classical RK4 chooses the symmetric structure to minimize the LTE. The most common parameter set is:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{1}{6}, \quad a_2 = \frac{1}{3}, \quad a_3 = \frac{1}{3}, \quad a_4 = \frac{1}{6} \\[4pt]
p_1 &= \frac{1}{2}, \quad p_2 = \frac{1}{2}, \quad p_3 = 1 \\[4pt]
q_{11} &= \frac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
q_{21} &= 0, \quad q_{22} = \frac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
q_{31} &= 0, \quad q_{32} = 0, \quad q_{33} = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Final RK4 Formulas (Classical):**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(x_n, y_n) \\[3pt]
k_2 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\ y_n + \tfrac{h}{2} k_1\right) \\[3pt]
k_3 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\ y_n + \tfrac{h}{2} k_2\right) \\[3pt]
k_4 &= f(x_n + h,\ y_n + h k_3) \\[3pt]
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{h}{6}\left(k_1 + 2 k_2 + 2 k_3 + k_4\right)
\end{aligned}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Truncation Error of RK4:**
> $$LTE = \frac{h^5}{120} y^{(5)}(\xi), \quad \xi \in (x_n, x_{n+1})$$
> Since $LTE = O(h^5)$, the method is of **order 4**. The global error after $N$ steps is $O(h^4)$.

**Step 5 — Verification (Order-Condition Check).** The weights $\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{6}$ sum to $1$ and they are chosen so that:
- The sum of weights of stages involving each derivative is correct.
- All conditions up to order $h^4$ are satisfied (8 conditions for order 4 with $s=4$).
- The error first appears at the $h^5$ term.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of RK3 (Classical Third-Order Runge–Kutta)

For completeness, the RK3 formula is:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(x_n, y_n) \\[3pt]
k_2 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\ y_n + \tfrac{h}{2} k_1\right) \\[3pt]
k_3 &= f(x_n + h,\ y_n - h k_1 + 2 h k_2) \\[3pt]
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{h}{6}\left(k_1 + 4 k_2 + k_3\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Local error: $O(h^4)$, Global error: $O(h^3)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — RK4 Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Input x0, y0, h, N] --> B[Initialize x = x0, y = y0]
    B --> C{i less than N?}
    C -- No --> Z[END: Output y final]
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute k1 = f of x, y]
    D --> E[Compute k2 = f of x + h/2, y + h/2 times k1]
    E --> F[Compute k3 = f of x + h/2, y + h/2 times k2]
    F --> G[Compute k4 = f of x + h, y + h times k3]
    G --> H[Update y = y + h/6 times k1 + 2k2 + 2k3 + k4]
    H --> I[Update x = x + h]
    I --> J[Increment i = i + 1]
    J --> C
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Comparison of RK Orders

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORDER1 [ORDER 1 METHODS]
        A1[Euler Method] --> A2[1 Stage]
        A2 --> A3[Accuracy O of h squared LTE]
    end

    subgraph ORDER2 [ORDER 2 METHODS]
        B1[RK2 Heun's] --> B2[2 Stages]
        B2 --> B3[Accuracy O of h cubed LTE]
        C1[RK2 Midpoint] --> C2[2 Stages]
        C2 --> C3[Accuracy O of h cubed LTE]
        D1[RK2 Ralston] --> D2[2 Stages]
        D2 --> D3[Accuracy O of h cubed LTE]
    end

    subgraph ORDER4 [ORDER 4 METHODS]
        E1[RK4 Classical] --> E2[4 Stages]
        E2 --> E3[Accuracy O of h to the 5th power LTE]
        E2 --> E4[Most widely used explicit RK]
        F1[RK45 Dormand-Prince] --> F2[6 Stages embedded pair]
        F2 --> F3[Adaptive step size control]
    end

    ORDER1 --> ORDER2
    ORDER2 --> ORDER4
    ORDER4 --> G1[Higher Order Implicit RK for Stiff Systems]
```

### 4.3 Slope-Sampling Visual Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[POINT P0 at xn, yn] --> S1[Stage 1: k1 at P0]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2: k2 at midpoint using k1]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: k3 at midpoint using k2]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: k4 at end-point P1 using k3]
    S4 --> W[Weighted Average: k1 plus 2k2 plus 2k3 plus k4 divided by 6]
    W --> P1[NEW POINT P1 at xn+1, yn+1]
    P1 --> P0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Schematic:**
> The diagram illustrates how RK4 takes **four slope samples** ($k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$) at strategic locations — the start, two midpoints, and the end of the interval — and combines them with weights $\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{6}$ to form a highly accurate weighted average slope. This averaged slope is then used to advance the solution from $P_0$ to $P_1$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*State the classical fourth-order Runge–Kutta formulas to find an approximate solution of $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$ at $x = x_0 + h$. Mention the order of the local truncation error.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The classical RK4 method gives:

$$k_1 = f(x_0, y_0)$$
$$k_2 = f\!\left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} k_1\right)$$
$$k_3 = f\!\left(x_0 + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_0 + \tfrac{h}{2} k_2\right)$$
$$k_4 = f(x_0 + h,\, y_0 + h k_3)$$
$$y_1 = y_0 + \frac{h}{6}\left(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4\right)$$

The order of the **local truncation error is $O(h^5)$**, making the method globally of order **4**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
*List any **two advantages** of the Runge–Kutta method over the Taylor series method for solving ODEs.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **No derivative computation required:** RK methods achieve high-order accuracy using only evaluations of $f(x,y)$ itself, avoiding the cumbersome computation of higher-order derivatives $\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}, \frac{d^3 y}{dx^3}, \dots$ required by Taylor series methods. **(1.5 Marks)**

2. **Self-starting / Single-step method:** Unlike multi-step methods, RK methods use only the previous value $y_n$ to compute $y_{n+1}$, requiring no auxiliary starter values. They can be applied directly from the initial condition. **(1.5 Marks)**

*(Bonus point may be awarded: better stability and larger step-size tolerance.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - Students frequently **forget to update $y_n$ and $x_n$** after each step — a 1-mark deduction per such slip.
> - In RK4, students often **swap $k_2$ and $k_3$** (using $k_2$ where $k_3$ is required) — this is a **major conceptual error** and can lead to **2-mark deduction**.
> - The **final weight $\frac{1}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)$** must be exactly followed; writing $\frac{1}{4}(k_1+k_2+k_3+k_4)$ (a common slip) loses **1 mark**.
> - Always carry **at least 4 decimal places** in intermediate computations.
> - Always **state the formula** before applying it — a 0.5-mark courtesy credit per sub-part.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Using the classical fourth-order Runge–Kutta method, find $y(0.2)$ and $y(0.4)$ given that $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y^2$ with $y(0) = 1$. Take step size $h = 0.2$.**

**Sub-Part (a) — Compute $y(0.2)$ using RK4. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

Given: $f(x,y) = x + y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.2$.

**Compute $k_1$:** $k_1 = f(0, 1) = 0 + (1)^2 = \mathbf{1.0000}$

**Compute $k_2$:** $k_2 = f\!\left(0 + 0.1,\ 1 + 0.1 \times 1.0000\right) = f(0.1, 1.1)$

$$k_2 = 0.1 + (1.1)^2 = 0.1 + 1.21 = \mathbf{1.3100}$$

**Compute $k_3$:** $k_3 = f\!\left(0.1,\ 1 + 0.1 \times 1.3100\right) = f(0.1, 1.1310)$

$$k_3 = 0.1 + (1.1310)^2 = 0.1 + 1.2792 = \mathbf{1.3792}$$

*(1.2792 computed as $1.131 \times 1.131 = 1.279161$, rounded to 1.2792)*

**Compute $k_4$:** $k_4 = f(0.2,\ 1 + 0.2 \times 1.3792) = f(0.2,\ 1.2758)$

$$k_4 = 0.2 + (1.2758)^2 = 0.2 + 1.6277 = \mathbf{1.8277}$$

*(1.2758² = 1.62767..., rounded to 1.6277)*

**Compute $y(0.2)$:**

$$y(0.2) = 1 + \frac{0.2}{6}\left(1.0000 + 2(1.3100) + 2(1.3792) + 1.8277\right)$$

$$= 1 + \frac{0.2}{6}\left(1.0000 + 2.6200 + 2.7584 + 1.8277\right)$$

$$= 1 + \frac{0.2}{6}(8.2061)$$

$$= 1 + 0.0333 \times 8.2061 = 1 + 0.2735$$

$$\boxed{y(0.2) \approx 1.2735}$$

**Sub-Part (b) — Compute $y(0.4)$ using RK4 with updated initial conditions. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

Updated: $x_1 = 0.2$, $y_1 = 1.2735$, $h = 0.2$.

**Compute $k_1$:** $k_1 = f(0.2, 1.2735) = 0.2 + (1.2735)^2 = 0.2 + 1.6218 = \mathbf{1.8218}$

**Compute $k_2$:** $k_2 = f\!\left(0.3,\ 1.2735 + 0.1 \times 1.8218\right) = f(0.3,\ 1.4557)$

$$k_2 = 0.3 + (1.4557)^2 = 0.3 + 2.1191 = \mathbf{2.4191}$$

**Compute $k_3$:** $k_3 = f\!\left(0.3,\ 1.2735 + 0.1 \times 2.4191\right) = f(0.3,\ 1.5154)$

$$k_3 = 0.3 + (1.5154)^2 = 0.3 + 2.2964 = \mathbf{2.5964}$$

**Compute $k_4$:** $k_4 = f(0.4,\ 1.2735 + 0.2 \times 2.5964) = f(0.4,\ 1.7928)$

$$k_4 = 0.4 + (1.7928)^2 = 0.4 + 3.2141 = \mathbf{3.6141}$$

**Compute $y(0.4)$:**

$$y(0.4) = 1.2735 + \frac{0.2}{6}\left(1.8218 + 2(2.4191) + 2(2.5964) + 3.6141\right)$$

$$= 1.2735 + \frac{0.2}{6}\left(1.8218 + 4.8382 + 5.1928 + 3.6141\right)$$

$$= 1.2735 + \frac{0.2}{6}(15.4669)$$

$$= 1.2735 + 0.0333 \times 15.4669$$

$$= 1.2735 + 0.5156$$

$$\boxed{y(0.4) \approx 1.7891}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Derive the second-order Runge–Kutta formula (Heun's method) for solving $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)$, $y(x_0) = y_0$. Hence, find $y(0.1)$ given $\frac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y$, $y(0) = 1$ with $h = 0.1$.**

**Sub-Part (a) — Derivation of RK2 (Heun's Method). (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume general 2-stage form:**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + a k_1 + b k_2$$

where
$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n), \quad k_2 = h f(x_n + \alpha h,\ y_n + \beta k_1)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the true solution by Taylor series:**

$$y(x_{n+1}) = y(x_n) + h y' + \frac{h^2}{2} y'' + \frac{h^3}{6} y''' + O(h^4)$$

With $y' = f$ and $y'' = f_x + f_y \cdot f$:

$$y(x_{n+1}) = y_n + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_x + f_y f) + \frac{h^3}{6}(\dots) + O(h^4)$$

**Step 3 — Expand $k_2$ using bivariate Taylor series:**

$$k_2 = h\left[f + \alpha h f_x + \beta k_1 f_y + O(h^2)\right]$$

$$= h f + \alpha h^2 f_x + \beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the increment:**

$$a k_1 + b k_2 = a h f + b h f + b\alpha h^2 f_x + b\beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

$$= (a+b) h f + b \alpha h^2 f_x + b \beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Step 5 — Match coefficients with the true Taylor expansion:**

| Power | Coefficient Comparison | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $h f$ | $a + b = 1$ | (i) |
| $h^2 f_x$ | $b \alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}$ | (ii) |
| $h^2 f f_y$ | $b \beta = \tfrac{1}{2}$ | (iii) |

**Step 6 — Solve.** Choosing $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$: from (ii) and (iii), $b = \tfrac{1}{2}$; from (i), $a = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Resulting Heun's Method:**

$$\boxed{\,k_1 = f(x_n, y_n), \quad k_2 = f(x_n + h,\ y_n + h k_1), \quad y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)\,}$$

This is of order $LTE = O(h^3)$ ⇒ **second-order accurate**.

**Sub-Part (b) — Apply Heun's method to $y' = x^2 + y$, $y(0) = 1$ at $x = 0.1$. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

Given: $f(x,y) = x^2 + y$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.1$.

**Compute $k_1$:** $k_1 = f(0, 1) = 0 + 1 = \mathbf{1.0000}$

**Compute $k_2$:** $k_2 = f(0.1, 1 + 0.1 \times 1) = f(0.1, 1.1)$

$$k_2 = (0.1)^2 + 1.1 = 0.01 + 1.1 = \mathbf{1.1100}$$

**Compute $y(0.1)$:**

$$y(0.1) = 1 + \frac{0.1}{2}(1.0000 + 1.1100) = 1 + 0.05 \times 2.1100$$

$$= 1 + 0.1055$$

$$\boxed{y(0.1) \approx 1.1055}$$

*(Note: Exact solution is $y = -x^2 - 2x - 2 + 5e^x$, giving $y(0.1) = 1.10517\dots$ — confirms high accuracy of Heun's method.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Checklist for Exam Day:**

- **Definition**: RK methods are single-step ODE solvers that use weighted averages of multiple stage-slope estimates — no derivative computations needed.
- **RK4 weights**: $\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{6}$ — sum to $1$; **must be exact**.
- **RK4 step locations**: $x_n, x_n + \frac{h}{2}, x_n + \frac{h}{2}, x_n + h$ — note that $k_2$ and $k_3$ are *both* at $x_n + \frac{h}{2}$ but use **different $y$-inputs**.
- **Order vs LTE**: RK4 has $LTE = O(h^5)$ and global error $O(h^4)$.
- **Euler is RK1** with $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$ and $y_{n+1} = y_n + h k_1$.
- **RK2 (Heun)**: $k_1$ at start, $k_2$ at end (predictor–corrector style).
- **RK2 (Midpoint)**: $k_1$ at start, $k_2$ at midpoint — often more accurate than Heun's for same $h$.
- **Why RK over Taylor?** Taylor needs derivatives; RK needs only $f(x,y)$ evaluations.
- **Stability region of RK4** is approximately the disk $\vert z \vert \le 2.785$ in the complex plane — large enough for most non-stiff biomedical ODEs.
- **Adaptive RK (RK45 Dormand–Prince)**: Uses 6 stages to produce both 4th- and 5th-order solutions, comparing them to estimate local error and adapt $h$ — basis of MATLAB's `ode45`.
- **Implicit vs Explicit**: All RK methods discussed (Euler, RK2, RK3, RK4) are **explicit**; implicit variants (Backward Euler, Trapezoidal) are required for **stiff** systems common in pharmacokinetics and neural dynamics.
- **Common pitfall**: Always update $y_n \to y_{n+1}$ and $x_n \to x_n + h$ **before** the next step — failing to do so propagates errors cumulatively.
- **Biomedical exam context**: Be prepared to apply RK4 to compartmental models (1-compartment, 2-compartment IV bolus drug models).
- **Quick verification trick**: Compare $y_{h}$ and $y_{h/2}$ using **Richardson extrapolation**: $y_{exact} \approx y_{h/2} + \frac{y_{h/2} - y_h}{2^p - 1}$, where $p$ is the order of the method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution of Linear Ordinary Differential Equations: Eigenvalue Methods; Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT744 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS TO BIOMEDICAL PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solution of Linear Ordinary Differential Equations: Eigenvalue Methods; Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear System of First-Order ODEs (State-Space Form):** A set of $n$ coupled first-order linear ordinary differential equations expressed compactly as
> $$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{F}(t)$$
> where $\mathbf{X}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector**, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **system (coefficient) matrix**, and $\mathbf{F}(t)$ is the forcing vector. When $\mathbf{F}(t) = \mathbf{0}$, the system is **homogeneous** and reduces to $\dfrac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\,\mathbf{X}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Eigenvalue Method (Modal Decomposition):** An analytical technique that decouples the coupled linear system by transforming it into the eigenbasis of $A$. If $A\mathbf{V} = \lambda \mathbf{V}$, the substitution $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{V}\,q$ converts the coupled system into independent scalar equations $\dot q = \lambda q$, whose solutions are elementary exponentials $q(t) = e^{\lambda t} q(0)$. The total solution is a linear combination of these **modes**.

The **matrix exponential** generalises the scalar exponential and is the closed-form solution:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{A(t-t_{0})}\,\mathbf{X}(t_{0})\;}$$

where $e^{A\tau}$ is defined by the convergent power series

$$e^{A\tau} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(A\tau)^{k}}{k!} = I + A\tau + \frac{A^{2}\tau^{2}}{2!} + \cdots$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra of Decoupled Voices"

Imagine an **orchestra** where every musician watches their neighbour and adjusts tempo to stay in sync (a *coupled* system). The **eigenvalue method** is like asking the conductor to step onto a *special stage* (the eigenbasis) where, surprisingly, each musician's score becomes completely independent — each one plays their own pure tone at their own natural pitch (the eigenvalue) with a fixed loudness pattern (the eigenvector). The conductor then translates the final sound back to the audience.

- **Matrix $A$** = the *coupling rules* between musicians
- **Eigenvector $\mathbf{V}$** = the *direction of pure motion* of each musician
- **Eigenvalue $\lambda$** = the *natural rate* (per second) at which that motion grows ($\lambda > 0$), decays ($\lambda < 0$), or oscillates ($\lambda$ complex)
- **Matrix exponential $e^{At}$** = the *time-evolution operator* that propagates the initial state forward

## 1.3 Why Eigenvalue Methods Matter in Biomedical Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Relevance:** Many biological processes — drug distribution across body compartments, infectious-disease progression, neuronal membrane dynamics, cardiac action-potential propagation, gene-regulation networks, and population dynamics of cells — are naturally modelled by systems of linear ODEs. The eigenvalue method provides (i) closed-form analytical solutions, (ii) stability criteria, and (iii) insight into the dominant *modes* governing long-term behaviour.

Standard physical/biological constants and metrics used in the analysis include:

- **Eigenvalue** $\lambda$ (units: $\text{s}^{-1}$ or $\text{min}^{-1}$ or $\text{hr}^{-1}$) — characteristic rate
- **Damping ratio** $\zeta$ — dimensionless measure of decay vs. oscillation
- **Natural frequency** $\omega_{n}$ (units: $\text{rad/s}$)
- **Spectral radius** $\rho(A) = \max \vert \lambda_{i}\vert$ — governs asymptotic growth/decay
- **Time constant** $\tau = 1/\vert \text{Re}(\lambda)\vert$ (units: s)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Setup

Consider the homogeneous linear system with constant coefficients

$$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = A\,\mathbf{X}, \qquad \mathbf{X}(0) = \mathbf{X}_{0}$$

**Step 1 — Assume an exponential ansatz.** Try a solution of the form $\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t}$, where $\mathbf{V} \neq \mathbf{0}$ is a constant column vector. Substitution yields

$$\lambda \mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t} = A\,\mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t} \;\Longrightarrow\; (A - \lambda I)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation.** A non-trivial solution $\mathbf{V}$ exists only when the matrix $A - \lambda I$ is singular:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This $n$-th degree polynomial in $\lambda$ yields $n$ eigenvalues $\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2},\dots,\lambda_{n}$ (counted with algebraic multiplicity, possibly complex).

**Step 3 — Eigenvectors.** For each $\lambda_{i}$, solve $(A - \lambda_{i} I)\mathbf{V}_{i} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 4 — General solution.** Superpose all linearly independent modal solutions:

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{i}\,\mathbf{V}_{i}\,e^{\lambda_{i} t}$$

The constants $c_{i}$ are determined from the initial condition $\mathbf{X}(0) = \mathbf{X}_{0}$.

## 2.2 Three Canonical Cases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The structure of the solution is dictated by the **Jordan canonical form** of $A$. There are three qualitatively different cases.

### Case A — Distinct Real Eigenvalues

When $A$ has $n$ linearly independent real eigenvectors $\mathbf{V}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{V}_{n}$ with real eigenvalues $\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{n}$:

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{i}\,\mathbf{V}_{i}\,e^{\lambda_{i} t}$$

The solution is a **pure exponential combination** — no oscillation, no polynomial multipliers.

### Case B — Complex Conjugate Eigenvalue Pairs

For a $2\times 2$ real block with $\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha \pm i\beta$ ($\beta \neq 0$) and complex eigenvector $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{P} + i\mathbf{Q}$:

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{\alpha t}\Big[ c_{1}(\mathbf{P}\cos\beta t - \mathbf{Q}\sin\beta t) + c_{2}(\mathbf{P}\sin\beta t + \mathbf{Q}\cos\beta t) \Big]$$

The motion is a **damped/growing sinusoid** — the hallmark of oscillatory biological systems (cardiac rhythms, neural firing, predator–prey cycles).

### Case C — Repeated Eigenvalues with Defect

If $\lambda$ has algebraic multiplicity $m > 1$ but geometric multiplicity $k < m$, we use **generalised eigenvectors** $\mathbf{W}$ defined by $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V}$. The associated solutions are

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = \big(c_{1}\mathbf{V} + c_{2}(\mathbf{V}\,t + \mathbf{W})\big)e^{\lambda t}, \quad \dots$$

The polynomial factor $t$ signals **resonance** — small forcing at the natural rate produces unbounded response.

## 2.3 Diagonalisation (When $A$ is Diagonalisable)

If $A$ has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors, the matrix $P = [\mathbf{V}_{1}\ \mathbf{V}_{2}\ \dots\ \mathbf{V}_{n}]$ satisfies

$$A = P D P^{-1}, \qquad D = \text{diag}(\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2},\dots,\lambda_{n})$$

The matrix exponential collapses to a diagonal matrix of exponentials:

$$\boxed{\;e^{At} = P\,\text{diag}(e^{\lambda_{1} t}, e^{\lambda_{2} t},\dots, e^{\lambda_{n} t})\,P^{-1}\;}$$

## 2.4 Cayley–Hamilton-Based Computation for $3\times 3$ Systems

By the Cayley–Hamilton theorem, $A$ satisfies its own characteristic equation $p(A) = 0$. For a $3\times 3$ matrix with characteristic polynomial

$$p(\lambda) = \lambda^{3} + a_{1}\lambda^{2} + a_{2}\lambda + a_{3}$$

we can write $e^{At}$ in the reduced form

$$e^{At} = \alpha_{0}(t)\,I + \alpha_{1}(t)\,A + \alpha_{2}(t)\,A^{2}$$

where the scalar functions $\alpha_{0},\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2}$ satisfy a linear system obtained by substituting the eigenvalues.

## 2.5 Stability Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Criterion:** The zero equilibrium of $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ is
> - **Asymptotically stable** $\iff$ $\text{Re}(\lambda_{i}) < 0$ for all $i$
> - **Marginally stable** $\iff$ $\text{Re}(\lambda_{i}) \le 0$ and any pure-imaginary eigenvalue has equal algebraic and geometric multiplicity
> - **Unstable** $\iff$ at least one $\text{Re}(\lambda_{i}) > 0$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ | Canonical homogeneous linear system |
| 2 | $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | Characteristic equation (eigenvalues) |
| 3 | $(A - \lambda_{i}I)\mathbf{V}_{i} = \mathbf{0}$ | Eigenvector definition |
| 4 | $\mathbf{X}(t) = \sum c_{i}\mathbf{V}_{i}e^{\lambda_{i}t}$ | General solution, distinct real $\lambda$ |
| 5 | $e^{At} = P\,\text{diag}(e^{\lambda_{1}t},\dots,e^{\lambda_{n}t})\,P^{-1}$ | Closed-form matrix exponential |
| 6 | $e^{A\tau} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(A\tau)^{k}}{k!}$ | Power-series definition |
| 7 | $\rho(A) = \max \vert \lambda_{i}\vert$ | Spectral radius (asymptotic growth rate) |
| 8 | $\tau_{i} = 1/\vert \text{Re}(\lambda_{i})\vert$ | Time constant for mode $i$ |
| 9 | $\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha \pm i\beta \;\Rightarrow\; e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{P}\cos\beta t - \mathbf{Q}\sin\beta t)$ | Oscillatory mode |
| 10 | $(A - \lambda I)^{m}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{0}$ | Generalised eigenvector of rank $m$ |
| 11 | $\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{A(t-t_{0})}\mathbf{X}(t_{0})$ | State-transition / evolution operator |
| 12 | $\alpha_{0}I + \alpha_{1}A + \alpha_{2}A^{2}$ | Cayley–Hamilton reduction of $e^{At}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Pharmacokinetics:** A two-compartment model of drug distribution yields a $2\times 2$ system whose eigenvalues identify the absorption and elimination rates directly.
- **Cardiac electrophysiology:** Hodgkin–Huxley linearisation around the resting state gives eigenvalues whose real parts determine whether the membrane returns to rest after perturbation.
- **Mechanical biomechanics:** A multi-link biomechanical model (e.g., a human arm) decouples into principal vibration modes whose eigenvalues are the natural frequencies.
- **Epidemic models (linearised SIR):** The dominant eigenvalue of the next-generation matrix dictates the basic reproduction number $\mathcal{R}_{0}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Existence of Eigen-Exp Solutions

**Claim.** The vector $\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t}$ solves $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ iff $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Proof.**

Assume $\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t}$ with constant $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$. Differentiate component-wise:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = \lambda\,\mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t}$$

Substitute into $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$:

$$\lambda\,\mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t} = A\,\mathbf{V}\,e^{\lambda t}$$

Cancel the non-zero scalar factor $e^{\lambda t}$:

$$\lambda\,\mathbf{V} = A\,\mathbf{V} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; A\mathbf{V} - \lambda I\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; (A - \lambda I)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$$

For a non-trivial $\mathbf{V} \neq \mathbf{0}$, the matrix $A - \lambda I$ must be singular, hence

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0 \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Real $2\times 2$ System with Complex Eigenvalues

Consider the system

$$\frac{d\mathbf{X}}{dt} = \begin{pmatrix} a & -b \\ b & a \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{X}, \qquad b \neq 0$$

**Step 1 — Characteristic polynomial.**

$$\det\!\begin{pmatrix} a-\lambda & -b \\ b & a-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (a-\lambda)^{2} + b^{2} = 0$$

Solving for $\lambda$:

$$(a-\lambda)^{2} = -b^{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; a - \lambda = \pm ib \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda = a \pm ib$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvector for $\lambda = a + ib$.**

Solve $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -ib & -b \\ b & -ib \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

From the first row: $-ib\,v_{1} - b\,v_{2} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; v_{2} = -i\,v_{1}$. Choosing $v_{1} = 1$:

$$\mathbf{V} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -i \end{pmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{P}} + i\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{Q}}$$

**Step 3 — Construct real solution.** The complex solution is

$$\mathbf{X}_{c}(t) = \mathbf{V}\,e^{(a+ib)t} = (\mathbf{P} + i\mathbf{Q})e^{at}(\cos bt + i\sin bt)$$

Expanding using Euler's formula and collecting real and imaginary parts (both are real solutions since $A$ is real):

$$\text{Re}\big[\mathbf{X}_{c}(t)\big] = e^{at}\big(\mathbf{P}\cos bt - \mathbf{Q}\sin bt\big) = e^{at}\begin{pmatrix} \cos bt \\ \sin bt \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\text{Im}\big[\mathbf{X}_{c}(t)\big] = e^{at}\big(\mathbf{P}\sin bt + \mathbf{Q}\cos bt\big) = e^{at}\begin{pmatrix} \sin bt \\ -\cos bt \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — General real solution.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{at}\!\left[ c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} \cos bt \\ \sin bt \end{pmatrix} + c_{2}\begin{pmatrix} \sin bt \\ -\cos bt \end{pmatrix} \right]\;}$$

**Physical interpretation.** The trajectory is a spiral in the phase plane. If $a < 0$, the spiral **decays** into the origin (stable focus). If $a > 0$, it **diverges** (unstable focus). If $a = 0$, it is a **closed orbit** (centre) — e.g., undamped oscillations in a predator–prey system.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Repeated Real Eigenvalue with One Missing Eigenvector

Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ (a $2\times 2$ Jordan block with $\lambda = 1$ of algebraic multiplicity 2).

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{pmatrix} 1-\lambda & 1 \\ 0 & 1-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (1-\lambda)^{2} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_{1} = \lambda_{2} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Find geometric multiplicity.** Solve $(A - I)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; v_{2} = 0,\; v_{1}\ \text{free}$$

So we obtain only **one** eigenvector $\mathbf{V} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$ — the matrix is defective.

**Step 3 — Find the generalised eigenvector $\mathbf{W}$.** Solve $(A - I)\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} w_{1} \\ w_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; w_{2} = 1,\; w_{1}\ \text{free}$$

Choose $w_{1} = 0$, so $\mathbf{W} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 4 — Construct the two linearly independent solutions.**

The standard modal ansatz gives only one solution $\mathbf{X}_{1}(t) = \mathbf{V}\,e^{t}$. For the second, substitute $\mathbf{X}_{2}(t) = (\mathbf{V}\,t + \mathbf{W})\,e^{t}$:

$$\dot{\mathbf{X}}_{2} = \mathbf{V}\,e^{t} + (\mathbf{V}\,t + \mathbf{W})\,e^{t} = \big[\mathbf{V}(t+1) + \mathbf{W}\big]e^{t}$$

Compute $A\mathbf{X}_{2} = A(\mathbf{V}\,t + \mathbf{W})e^{t}$. Since $A\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V}$ and $A\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V} + \mathbf{W}$ (from $(A-I)\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V}$):

$$A\mathbf{X}_{2} = (A\mathbf{V}\,t + A\mathbf{W})e^{t} = (\mathbf{V}\,t + \mathbf{V} + \mathbf{W})e^{t} = \big[\mathbf{V}(t+1) + \mathbf{W}\big]e^{t}$$

The two expressions are identical, confirming $\mathbf{X}_{2}(t)$ is a solution.

**Step 5 — General solution.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{X}(t) = c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}e^{t} + c_{2}\!\left[\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}t + \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\right]e^{t}\;}$$

The polynomial factor $t$ in the second mode is the signature of a defective matrix.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Two-Compartment Pharmacokinetic Model

**Model.** Let $x_{1}(t), x_{2}(t)$ be the drug masses in the **central** and **peripheral** compartments. The system is

$$\frac{dx_{1}}{dt} = -(k_{12} + k_{el})x_{1} + k_{21}\,x_{2}, \qquad \frac{dx_{2}}{dt} = k_{12}\,x_{1} - k_{21}\,x_{2}$$

In matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ with

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} -(k_{12}+k_{el}) & k_{21} \\ k_{12} & -k_{21} \end{pmatrix}$$

where $k_{el}$ is the elimination rate constant, $k_{12}$ is the central-to-peripheral transfer rate, and $k_{21}$ is the peripheral-to-central transfer rate (units: $\text{h}^{-1}$).

**Characteristic equation:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^{2} + (k_{12}+k_{el}+k_{21})\lambda + k_{el}\,k_{21} = 0$$

By Vieta's formulas, $\lambda_{1} + \lambda_{2} = -(k_{12}+k_{el}+k_{21}) < 0$ and $\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2} = k_{el}k_{21} > 0$. Since the sum is negative and the product is positive, **both eigenvalues are real and negative** — the drug always clears. The two negative eigenvalues are the **distribution rate** (fast mode) and the **elimination rate** (slow mode).

**Steady state.** Since $\text{Re}(\lambda_{i}) < 0$, $\mathbf{X}(t) \to \mathbf{0}$ as $t \to \infty$ — the system is asymptotically stable and the drug is eventually eliminated.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Closed-Form via Cayley–Hamilton for $3\times 3$

For a $3\times 3$ system with distinct eigenvalues $\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2},\lambda_{3}$, write

$$e^{At} = \alpha_{0}(t)I + \alpha_{1}(t)A + \alpha_{2}(t)A^{2}$$

The Cayley–Hamilton relation $A^{3} = -a_{1}A^{2} - a_{2}A - a_{3}I$ (where $a_{k}$ are coefficients of the characteristic polynomial) reduces the power series. Substituting the eigenvalues, each $\lambda_{i}$ must satisfy

$$e^{\lambda_{i} t} = \alpha_{0} + \alpha_{1}\lambda_{i} + \alpha_{2}\lambda_{i}^{2}, \qquad i = 1,2,3$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & \lambda_{1} & \lambda_{1}^{2} \\ 1 & \lambda_{2} & \lambda_{2}^{2} \\ 1 & \lambda_{3} & \lambda_{3}^{2} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} \alpha_{0} \\ \alpha_{1} \\ \alpha_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} e^{\lambda_{1}t} \\ e^{\lambda_{2}t} \\ e^{\lambda_{3}t} \end{pmatrix}$$

This is a **Vandermonde system** (the matrix is invertible for distinct $\lambda_{i}$), and inverting it gives the closed-form $e^{At}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Solution Pipeline of the Eigenvalue Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System dX/dt = A X] --> B[Form Matrix A and Initial Condition X0]
    B --> C[Compute Characteristic Equation det A minus lambda I = 0]
    C --> D{Eigenvalues Distinct?}
    D -- Yes Real --> E1[Solve A minus lambda I times V = 0 for each V]
    E1 --> F1[General Solution X of t = sum c_i V_i exp lambda_i t]
    D -- Yes Complex Conjugate --> E2[Take Real and Imaginary Parts of Complex V]
    E2 --> F2[Real Solution X of t = exp alpha t times P cos beta t minus Q sin beta t]
    D -- No Repeated --> E3[Find Generalised Eigenvectors W from A minus lambda I times W = V]
    E3 --> F3[Solution Includes Polynomial in t times exp lambda t]
    F1 --> G[Apply Initial Condition X at 0 = X0 to Find Constants c_i]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Final Closed Form X of t = e to the power At times X0]
    H --> I[Stability Check: Re lambda less than 0 for All i]
    I --> J[End: Interpret Modes in Biomedical Context]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two-Compartment Pharmacokinetic Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        P0[Initial Drug Dose x1 at 0]
        P1[Initial Peripheral x2 at 0]
    end
    subgraph ModelLayer[Model Layer]
        MA[Matrix A from Rate Constants k12, k21, kel]
        ME[Matrix Exponential e to the power A t]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer[Compute Layer]
        CE[Characteristic Polynomial]
        EV[Eigenvalues lambda1, lambda2]
        EG[Eigenvectors V1, V2]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        SX[State X of t in Central Compartment]
        SY[State Y of t in Peripheral Compartment]
        ST[Stability and Time Constants tau1, tau2]
    end
    P0 --> MA
    P1 --> MA
    MA --> CE
    CE --> EV
    EV --> EG
    EG --> ME
    ME --> SX
    ME --> SY
    EV --> ST
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual — Eigenvalue Spectrum and System Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Eigenvalue lambda1] --> CL1{Real Part Sign}
    L2[Eigenvalue lambda2] --> CL2{Real Part Sign}
    L3[Eigenvalue lambda3 Complex] --> CL3{Real Part Sign}
    CL1 -- Positive --> UN1[Unstable Growth Mode]
    CL1 -- Zero --> MN1[Marginal Mode]
    CL1 -- Negative --> ST1[Stable Decay Mode]
    CL2 -- Positive --> UN2[Unstable Growth Mode]
    CL2 -- Zero --> MN2[Marginal Mode]
    CL2 -- Negative --> ST2[Stable Decay Mode]
    CL3 -- Imaginary Only --> OSC1[Pure Oscillation Centre]
    CL3 -- Negative Real --> SP1[Decaying Spiral Stable Focus]
    CL3 -- Positive Real --> SP2[Growing Spiral Unstable Focus]
```

## 4.4 Description of the Physical Schematic

The diagrams above map the **computational pipeline** of the eigenvalue method:

1. **Input Layer** receives the system's coefficient matrix $A$ and initial state $\mathbf{X}_{0}$.
2. **Compute Layer** finds the eigenvalues by solving the characteristic equation, then finds eigenvectors by null-space computation. This is the "modal analysis" stage.
3. **Model Layer** assembles the matrix exponential $e^{At}$ using either diagonalisation (if $A$ is diagonalisable) or the Cayley–Hamilton reduction (always valid).
4. **Output Layer** produces the time evolution of every state variable and the stability metrics (time constants, oscillation frequencies, decay rates).

In the **biomedical context**, the state variables typically represent drug concentrations, cell populations, ion concentrations, or stress–strain measures. The dominant eigenvalue (one with the largest real part) dictates the **long-term behaviour** of the biological process.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Cayley–Hamilton theorem and explain its role in computing the matrix exponential $e^{At}$ for a $3 \times 3$ system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Cayley–Hamilton Theorem:** Every square matrix $A$ satisfies its own characteristic polynomial. If $p(\lambda) = \lambda^{n} + a_{1}\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n}$, then $p(A) = A^{n} + a_{1}A^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n}I = 0$.
> **Role in $e^{At}$:** Because $A^{n}$ can be expressed as a linear combination of $I, A, A^{2}, \dots, A^{n-1}$, the infinite power series of $e^{At}$ collapses to a finite sum $e^{At} = \alpha_{0}(t)I + \alpha_{1}(t)A + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}(t)A^{n-1}$. The scalar functions $\alpha_{k}(t)$ are found by solving a small linear system (Vandermonde for distinct $\lambda$). **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**For the system $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ with $A = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, write down the eigenvalues and determine the stability of the equilibrium at the origin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The matrix $A$ is upper triangular, so its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries: $\lambda_{1} = -2$ and $\lambda_{2} = -1$.
> Since both eigenvalues have strictly negative real parts, the equilibrium at the origin is **asymptotically stable**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two internal choices.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Hence obtain the general solution of $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (4 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - (-1)(1) = \lambda^{2} - 6\lambda + 9 = (\lambda - 3)^{2} = 0$$

Repeated eigenvalue $\lambda = 3$ (algebraic multiplicity 2). **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Eigenvector.** Solve $(A - 3I)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; v_{1} + v_{2} = 0$$

Choose $\mathbf{V} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$. Geometric multiplicity is 1 — matrix is **defective**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Generalised eigenvector.** Solve $(A - 3I)\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} w_{1} \\ w_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; w_{1} + w_{2} = 1$$

Choose $w_{1} = 1, w_{2} = 0$, so $\mathbf{W} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — General solution.**

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{3t} + c_{2}\!\left[\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}t + \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\right]e^{3t}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{3t}\!\left[\,c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + c_{2}\!\left(t\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\right)\right]\;} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**(b)** A two-compartment drug model is described by
$$\dot{x}_{1} = -3x_{1} + x_{2}, \qquad \dot{x}_{2} = 2x_{1} - 2x_{2}, \qquad x_{1}(0) = 4,\ x_{2}(0) = 1$$
Solve the system using the eigenvalue method and interpret the long-term behaviour. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Matrix form.** $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ with $A = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{X}_{0} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (-3 - \lambda)(-2 - \lambda) - 2 = \lambda^{2} + 5\lambda + 4 = (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 4) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1} = -1$, $\lambda_{2} = -4$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Eigenvectors.**
For $\lambda_{1} = -1$: $\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{1} = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{V}_{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

For $\lambda_{2} = -4$: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{2} = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{V}_{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Apply initial conditions.**

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}e^{-t} + c_{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{-4t}$$

At $t = 0$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + c_{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

So $c_{1} + c_{2} = 4$ and $2c_{1} - c_{2} = 1$. Adding: $3c_{1} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; c_{1} = 5/3$, $c_{2} = 7/3$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Final solution and interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{X}(t) = \frac{5}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}e^{-t} + \frac{7}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{-4t}\;}$$

Since both eigenvalues are real and negative, $\mathbf{X}(t) \to \mathbf{0}$ as $t \to \infty$: the drug is **completely eliminated** from both compartments. The slow mode ($e^{-t}$, time constant 1 hour) governs the long-term decay; the fast mode ($e^{-4t}$, time constant 0.25 hour) is the rapid initial distribution phase. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks)** (Internal Choice)

**(a)** For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, find the eigenvalues and the general real solution of $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$. Identify the type of equilibrium and discuss stability. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^{2} + 4 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_{1,2} = \pm 2i$$

Purely imaginary eigenvalues — the equilibrium is a **centre**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Eigenvector for $\lambda = 2i$.** Solve $(A - 2iI)\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -2i & -2 \\ 2 & -2i \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; -2i\,v_{1} = 2v_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; v_{2} = -i\,v_{1}$$

Take $v_{1} = 1$: $\mathbf{V} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -i \end{pmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{P}} + i\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{Q}}$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Real solutions.** With $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 2$:

$$\text{Re}[\mathbf{V}e^{2it}] = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 2t \\ \sin 2t \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \text{Im}[\mathbf{V}e^{2it}] = \begin{pmatrix} \sin 2t \\ -\cos 2t \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — General solution.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{X}(t) = c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} \cos 2t \\ \sin 2t \end{pmatrix} + c_{2}\begin{pmatrix} \sin 2t \\ -\cos 2t \end{pmatrix}\;}$$

The trajectories are **closed elliptical orbits** around the origin in the phase plane. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Stability.** $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$ for both eigenvalues, and they are simple. The equilibrium is **Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable** — perturbations neither grow nor decay; the system oscillates indefinitely with frequency $\omega = 2\ \text{rad/s}$. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A compartmental model for iodine distribution in the thyroid gland is given by
$$\dot{x}_{1} = -0.5x_{1} + 0.2x_{2}, \qquad \dot{x}_{2} = 0.5x_{1} - 0.2x_{2}, \qquad x_{1}(0) = 10,\ x_{2}(0) = 0$$
Solve the system and determine the percentage of initial dose remaining at $t = 5$ hours. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Matrix form.** $A = \begin{pmatrix} -0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & -0.2 \end{pmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^{2} + 0.7\lambda + 0 = \lambda(\lambda + 0.7) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1} = 0$, $\lambda_{2} = -0.7$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Eigenvectors.**
For $\lambda_{1} = 0$: $A\mathbf{V}_{1} = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; -0.5v_{1} + 0.2v_{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; v_{2} = 2.5 v_{1}$. Take $\mathbf{V}_{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

For $\lambda_{2} = -0.7$: $(A + 0.7I)\mathbf{V}_{2} = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{2} = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; v_{1} + v_{2} = 0$. Take $\mathbf{V}_{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Apply initial conditions.**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = c_{1}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + c_{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$2c_{1} + c_{2} = 10$ and $5c_{1} - c_{2} = 0$. Adding: $7c_{1} = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; c_{1} = 10/7$, $c_{2} = 50/7$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Solution and evaluation at $t = 5$.**

$$\mathbf{X}(5) = \frac{10}{7}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + \frac{50}{7}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{-3.5}$$

Numerically, $e^{-3.5} \approx 0.0302$:

$$x_{1}(5) \approx \frac{20}{7} + \frac{50}{7}(0.0302) \approx 2.857 + 0.216 = 3.073$$

$$x_{2}(5) \approx \frac{50}{7} - \frac{50}{7}(0.0302) \approx 7.143 - 0.216 = 6.927$$

**Total dose remaining** $= x_{1} + x_{2} \approx 3.073 + 6.927 = 10.0$. Since $\lambda_{1} = 0$, the system has a **non-trivial steady state** — the system conserves the total mass in the limit, redistributing it between compartments but never eliminating it. The percentage remaining is **100%** of the dose, but it is now distributed as ~30.7% in the central compartment and ~69.3% in the peripheral compartment. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take the real part of complex eigenvectors** when the system is real. (Loses 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Missing the polynomial factor $t$** in the repeated-eigenvalue case — students often write only $c\,\mathbf{V}e^{\lambda t}$ and lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in applying initial conditions** — always substitute $t = 0$ first, set up the linear system, and solve it carefully.
> 4. **Conflating algebraic and geometric multiplicities** — check whether $(A - \lambda I)$ has full nullity before assuming diagonalisability.
> 5. **Stating the wrong stability conclusion** — eigenvalues on the imaginary axis need a *separate* check for marginal vs asymptotic stability.
> 6. **Forgetting the units of eigenvalues** in biomedical problems — the eigenvalue's reciprocal is the time constant, and the question often asks for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- The system $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A\mathbf{X}$ has solution $\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{A(t-t_{0})}\mathbf{X}(t_{0})$.
- The **characteristic equation** is $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$.
- **Distinct real eigenvalues** → sum of exponentials $\sum c_{i}\mathbf{V}_{i}e^{\lambda_{i}t}$.
- **Complex conjugate pair** $\alpha \pm i\beta$ → damped oscillation $e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{P}\cos\beta t - \mathbf{Q}\sin\beta t)$.
- **Repeated eigenvalue with defect** → polynomial-in-$t$ modes via generalised eigenvectors.
- **Diagonalisation:** $A = PDP^{-1} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{At} = P\,\text{diag}(e^{\lambda_{1}t},\dots,e^{\lambda_{n}t})\,P^{-1}$.
- **Cayley–Hamilton:** reduces $e^{At}$ to a polynomial in $A$ of degree $\le n-1$.
- **Stability** is decided entirely by $\text{sign}(\text{Re}(\lambda_{i}))$ for all $i$.
- **Spectral radius** $\rho(A) = \max \vert \lambda_{i}\vert$ governs asymptotic growth.
- **Time constant** $\tau_{i} = 1/\vert \text{Re}(\lambda_{i})\vert$.
- **Biomedical interpretation:** eigenvalues = characteristic rates; eigenvectors = distribution patterns; time constants = clinically relevant durations.
- Always check **initial conditions** to find the constants $c_{i}$.
- Always check whether $A$ is **diagonalisable** before assuming a clean modal decomposition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glycolysis Pathways of Living Cells; Dynamics of Membrane and Nerve Cell Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT744 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS TO BIOMEDICAL PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Glycolysis Pathways of Living Cells; Dynamics of Membrane and Nerve Cell Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Glycolysis — The Sugar-Cleaving Engine

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Glycolysis is the **ubiquitous, oxygen-independent cytosolic metabolic pathway** that catabolizes a single six-carbon glucose molecule ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) into two molecules of three-carbon pyruvate through a sequence of **ten enzyme-catalyzed reactions**, yielding a net gain of **2 ATP**, **2 NADH**, and **2 H₂O**. It is the foundational entry point of cellular respiration, feeding into the TCA cycle, fermentation, or anaerobic respiration depending on oxygen availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Computational treatment of glycolysis requires formulation of reaction kinetics using **Michaelis-Menten formalism**, **mass-action rate laws**, and **steady-state flux balance** under the assumption of a well-mixed cytoplasmic compartment.

**Overall Stoichiometry:**

$$Glucose + 2\,NAD^+ + 2\,ADP + 2\,P_i \rightarrow 2\,Pyruvate + 2\,NADH + 2\,ATP + 2\,H_2O + 2\,H^+$$

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of glycolysis as a **factory assembly line that disassembles a large product (glucose) into smaller packages (pyruvate) and recoups energy as a "byproduct currency" (ATP/NADH)**. The factory has two shifts: the **Investment Phase** (first 5 steps) where the factory actually *spends* 2 ATP to set up the line and break the molecule in half, and the **Payoff Phase** (last 5 steps) where it earns back 4 ATP + 2 NADH per glucose, leaving a net profit of 2 ATP.

### 1.2 Dynamics of Membrane and Nerve Cell Potentials

**Formal Definition:**
The **membrane potential** ($V_m$) is the time-dependent voltage difference across a neuron's lipid bilayer, governed by ionic concentration gradients, selective permeability, and active ion pumps. The **nerve action potential** is a self-propagating, transient depolarization wave produced by voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channel dynamics, classically described by the **Hodgkin–Huxley (HH) formalism** (1952 Nobel Prize).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constants for Nerve Cell Modeling:**
> - Faraday Constant: $\mathbf{F = 96{,}485\ C\cdot mol^{-1}}$
> - Universal Gas Constant: $\mathbf{R = 8.314\ J\cdot mol^{-1}\cdot K^{-1}}$
> - Body Temperature: $\mathbf{T = 310\ K}$ (37 °C)
> - RT/F at 37 °C: **~26.73 mV**

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a **water reservoir separated by a wall with controllable sluice gates**. The water level difference represents the voltage (charge gradient). The **sodium-potassium pump** is a motor that continuously pumps water back uphill. When a stimulus opens the Na⁺ gates, water rushes in (depolarization); when K⁺ gates open, water rushes out (repolarization). The **HH equations** mathematically describe how the gates open and close in time.

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently tests the **Nernst Equation**, the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation**, and the qualitative shape of the action potential waveform. Memorize the standard ionic concentrations for mammalian neurons.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Glycolysis — The Ten Sequential Steps

#### Phase I: Energy Investment (Hexokinase → Aldolase)
| Step | Substrate | Enzyme | Product | ATP/NADH | ATP Δ |
|------|-----------|--------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | Glucose | Hexokinase (HK) | Glucose-6-P | -1 ATP | Invest |
| 2 | G6P | Phosphoglucose Isomerase (PGI) | Fructose-6-P | 0 | — |
| 3 | F6P | Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) | Fructose-1,6-BP | -1 ATP | Invest |
| 4 | F1,6BP | Aldolase | DHAP + G3P | 0 | Split |
| 5 | DHAP | Triose Phosphate Isomerase (TIM) | G3P (×2) | 0 | — |

#### Phase II: Energy Payoff (G3P → Pyruvate)
| Step | Substrate | Enzyme | Product | NADH/ATP | ATP Δ |
|------|-----------|--------|---------|----------|-------|
| 6 | G3P | Glyceraldehyde-3-P Dehydrogenase (GAPDH) | 1,3-BPG | +NADH | 0 |
| 7 | 1,3-BPG | Phosphoglycerate Kinase (PGK) | 3-PG | +1 ATP | Earn |
| 8 | 3-PG | Phosphoglycerate Mutase (PGM) | 2-PG | 0 | — |
| 9 | 2-PG | Enolase | PEP | 0 | — |
| 10 | PEP | Pyruvate Kinase (PK) | Pyruvate | +1 ATP | Earn |

**Net Per Glucose:** 4 ATP produced − 2 ATP consumed = **2 ATP**; 2 NADH produced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three **irreversible regulatory steps** (1, 3, 10) are catalyzed by HK, PFK-1, and PK. These are the **kinetic bottlenecks** and the focus of computational flux control analysis in KTU problems.

### 2.2 Computational Kinetics of Glycolysis

**Michaelis–Menten Rate Law** (single substrate, irreversible step):

$$v = \frac{V_{max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

where $V_{max} = k_{cat}\,[E]_T$ and $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1}+k_{cat}}{k_1}$.

**Mass-Action Reversible Rate Law:**

$$v = k_f[S] - k_r[P]$$

**Steady-State Flux Balance (for pathway segment $i$):**

$$J_i = J_{i+1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = v_2 = \cdots = v_{10}$$

**PFK-1 Allosteric Regulation (Standard Model):**

$$v_{PFK} = \frac{V_{max}\,[F6P]^n}{K_m^{app} + [F6P]^n}$$

with Hill coefficient $n \approx 4$ (cooperative, ATP/AMP/citrate modulated).

### 2.3 Membrane and Nerve Cell Potentials — KTU Formula Sheet

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Nernst (single ion) | $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Equilibrium potential for ion $X$ |
| Nernst (log₁₀) | $E_X = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Computational convenience |
| GHK Voltage | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Resting membrane potential |
| GHK (form) | $V_m \approx 61\,\log_{10}\dfrac{[K^+]_o + 0.04[Na^+]_o}{[K^+]_i + 0.04[Na^+]_i}$ mV | Mammalian neuron (T=37°C) |
| Nernst-Planck Flux | $J = -D\dfrac{d[X]}{dx} - \dfrac{zF}{RT}\,D\,[X]\dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | Ion transport through membrane |
| HH Capacitive | $C_m\dfrac{dV}{dt} = - (I_{Na} + I_K + I_L)$ | Membrane total current |
| HH Na⁺ current | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\,m^3 h\,(V - E_{Na})$ | Voltage-gated Na⁺ |
| HH K⁺ current | $I_K = \bar{g}_K\,n^4\,(V - E_K)$ | Voltage-gated K⁺ |
| Gating variable | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V)x$ | $x \in \{m, h, n\}$ |
| Cable equation | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{r_m c_m}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{V}{r_m}$ | Spatial propagation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The GHK equation uses **inverted chloride terms** ($[Cl^-]_i$ in numerator, $[Cl^-]_o$ in denominator) because chloride is a **negative ion** ($z = -1$). Many students flip this and lose marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Process Engineering:** Glycolysis flux models inform **bioreactor design** for yeast-based bioethanol production and mammalian cell culture optimization in pharmaceutical manufacturing.
- **Neural Engineering & Prosthetics:** HH and cable equations underpin the design of **deep brain stimulators (DBS)**, **cochlear implants**, and **retinal prosthetics**.
- **Pharmacology:** The Nernst and GHK equations are critical for modeling **drug–ion channel interactions** and anesthetic action.
- **ECG/EEG Signal Processing:** Action potential summation forms the biophysical basis of clinical electrophysiology diagnostics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Single-Ion Equilibrium Potential)

Starting from electrochemical potential equality across a membrane:

**Step 1: Define the electrochemical potential of ion $X$ of charge $z$ on side $i$ and side $o$.**

$$\tilde{\mu}_X^{i} = \mu_X^0 + RT\ln[X]_i + zF V_i$$

$$\tilde{\mu}_X^{o} = \mu_X^0 + RT\ln[X]_o + zF V_o$$

**Step 2: At electrochemical equilibrium, $\tilde{\mu}_X^{i} = \tilde{\mu}_X^{o}$.**

$$\mu_X^0 + RT\ln[X]_i + zF V_i = \mu_X^0 + RT\ln[X]_o + zF V_o$$

**Step 3: Cancel $\mu_X^0$ and rearrange.**

$$RT\ln[X]_i - RT\ln[X]_o = zF V_o - zF V_i$$

$$RT\ln\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = zF(V_o - V_i)$$

**Step 4: Define the membrane potential $E_X \equiv V_i - V_o$ (inside minus outside).**

$$V_o - V_i = -E_X$$

$$RT\ln\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = -zF\,E_X$$

**Step 5: Solve for $E_X$ and invert the log ratio.**

$$E_X = -\frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** for a single permeant ion species.

**Step 6: Convert to log₁₀ using $\ln(x) = 2.303\,\log_{10}(x)$.**

$$E_X = \frac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

**Step 7: Evaluate at body temperature T = 310 K:**

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.06153\ V \approx 61.5\ mV$$

$$E_X = \frac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\ mV$$

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

Given mammalian neuron: $[K^+]_i = 140\ mM$, $[K^+]_o = 5\ mM$, $z = +1$, T = 37 °C.

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\frac{5}{140} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03571)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.4472$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4472) = -89.0\ mV$$

> **Expected KTU Answer:** $E_K \approx -89$ to $-90$ mV.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

This extends the Nernst equation to a membrane permeable to multiple ions simultaneously.

**Step 1: Start from the Nernst-Planck flux for ion $X$ at steady state, integrating across membrane width $\delta$:**

$$J_X = -P_X\,z_XF\,\frac{[X]_i\,e^{z_XFV/RT} - [X]_o}{e^{z_XFV/RT} - 1}$$

**Step 2: Apply the GHK condition: net current is zero (steady state, $I_{total} = 0$).**

$$I = F(J_{K^+} + J_{Na^+} - J_{Cl^-}) = 0$$

where the negative sign for $Cl^-$ arises because $z_{Cl} = -1$.

**Step 3: Substitute each flux and solve for the constant-field voltage $V = V_m$:**

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F}\ln\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

**Step 4: Apply typical mammalian permeability ratios $P_{Na}:P_K:P_{Cl} = 1:1:0.4$ for muscle, or $1:25:0.4$ for squid axon. For neurons $P_K \gg P_{Na}$:**

$$V_m \approx \frac{RT}{F}\ln\frac{[K^+]_o + 0.04[Na^+]_o + 0.4[Cl^-]_i}{[K^+]_i + 0.04[Na^+]_i + 0.4[Cl^-]_o}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Example — GHK Resting Potential

Given: $[K^+]_i = 140$, $[K^+]_o = 5$, $[Na^+]_i = 15$, $[Na^+]_o = 145$, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$, $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM. Use $P_{Na}:P_K:P_{Cl} = 1:1:0.4$.

**Numerator:** $5 + 0.04(145) + 0.4(4) = 5 + 5.8 + 1.6 = 12.4$

**Denominator:** $140 + 0.04(15) + 0.4(120) = 140 + 0.6 + 48 = 188.6$

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\frac{12.4}{188.6} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.06575)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.06575) = -1.1822$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1822) = -72.7\ mV$$

> **Expected KTU Answer:** $V_m \approx -70$ to $-75$ mV (consistent with measured resting potential).

### 3.5 Michaelis–Menten Derivation (Quick-Equilibrium Assumption)

**Step 1: Elementary reaction scheme for enzyme $E$ and substrate $S$:**

$$E + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} ES \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} E + P$$

**Step 2: Mass conservation: $[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$.**

**Step 3: Steady-state on $[ES]$:**

$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1[E][S] - (k_{-1}+k_{cat})[ES] = 0$$

**Step 4: Substitute $[E] = [E]_T - [ES]$ and solve.**

$$[ES] = \frac{[E]_T[S]}{K_m + [S]}\quad\text{where }K_m = \frac{k_{-1}+k_{cat}}{k_1}$$

**Step 5: Initial rate $v_0 = k_{cat}[ES]$:**

$$v_0 = \frac{k_{cat}[E]_T[S]}{K_m + [S]} = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

### 3.6 ATP Yield Accounting for NADH (Computational Bioenergetics)

**Step 1:** Glycolysis produces 2 NADH per glucose in cytosol.
**Step 2:** Each cytosolic NADH yields ~1.5–2.5 ATP via the **malate-aspartate** or **glycerol-phosphate shuttle**.
**Step 3:** Total ATP per glucose (anaerobic): $2\ \text{ATP (substrate-level)} = 2\ \text{ATP net}$.
**Step 4:** Total ATP per glucose (aerobic, including TCA + oxidative phosphorylation): $2 + 5(2.5) + 2(2.5) = 2 + 12.5 + 5 = \approx 30$–**32 ATP**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU may ask you to compute the **P/O ratio** or perform an **ATP balance** across the full pathway. Show stoichiometric tables for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Glycolysis Pathway — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GLUCOSE] --> B[Hexokinase]
    B --> C[Glucose-6-Phosphate]
    C --> D[PGI Isomerase]
    D --> E[Fructose-6-Phosphate]
    E --> F[PFK-1 Phosphofructokinase]
    F --> G[Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphate]
    G --> H[Aldolase]
    H --> I[DHAP]
    H --> J[G3P]
    I --> K[TIM Isomerase]
    K --> J
    J --> L[GAPDH]
    L --> M[1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate]
    M --> N[PGK]
    N --> O[3-Phosphoglycerate]
    O --> P[PGM Mutase]
    P --> Q[2-Phosphoglycerate]
    Q --> R[Enolase]
    R --> S[Phosphoenolpyruvate]
    S --> T[Pyruvate Kinase]
    T --> U[PYRUVATE x2]
```

### 4.2 Nerve Action Potential — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[RESTING STATE -70 mV] -->|Stimulus threshold| D[DEPOLARIZATION Na+ INFLUX]
    D --> P[PEAK +30 to +40 mV]
    P --> RP[REPOLARIZATION K+ EFFLUX]
    RP --> HY[HYPERPOLARIZATION -80 mV]
    HY --> R
    subgraph IONIC CURRENTS
    NA[Na+ channels open] -->|m^3 h| D
    KX[K+ channels open] -->|n^4| RP
    LK[Leak Cl-] --> R
    end
```

### 4.3 Computational Glycolysis Flux — Michaelis-Menten Module Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Glucose Input Gt] --> E1[Step 1 Hexokinase HK]
    E1 --> E2[Step 2 PGI]
    E2 --> E3[Step 3 PFK-1 RATE LIMITING]
    E3 --> E4[Step 4 Aldolase]
    E4 --> E5[Step 5 TIM]
    E5 --> E6[Step 6 GAPDH]
    E6 --> E7[Step 7 PGK]
    E7 --> E8[Step 8 PGM]
    E8 --> E9[Step 9 Enolase]
    E9 --> E10[Step 10 PK RATE LIMITING]
    E10 --> OUT1[Pyruvate Output]
    OUT1 --> OUT2[2 ATP NET]
    OUT1 --> OUT3[2 NADH]
```

### 4.4 Hodgkin-Huxley Computational Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STIM[Stimulus Current Istim] --> SUM((Sum I total))
    C[Capacitive Current Cm dV/dt] --> SUM
    SUM --> V[Membrane Voltage Vm]
    V --> NA[Ion Channel Na activation m]
    V --> NA2[Ion Channel Na inactivation h]
    V --> K[Ion Channel K activation n]
    NA --> INA[INa = gNa m^3 h V - ENa]
    K --> IK[IK = gK n^4 V - EK]
    NA2 --> INA
    L[Leak Channels] --> IL[IL = gL V - EL]
    INA --> SUM
    IK --> SUM
    IL --> SUM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the Nernst Equation and state its significance in determining the resting membrane potential of a neuron.**

**Model Answer:**
The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential across a permeable membrane for a single ionic species $X$ of valence $z$:

$$E_X = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

At body temperature (310 K), $E_X = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ mV. **Significance:** It defines the membrane voltage at which there is no net flow of ion $X$. For a real neuron permeable to multiple ions, the resting potential is a weighted average (GHK) of the individual Nernst potentials, heavily dominated by $E_K \approx -89$ mV.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List the three rate-limiting (irreversible) steps of glycolysis and name the corresponding regulatory enzymes.**

**Model Answer:**
The three physiologically irreversible regulatory steps of glycolysis are:

1. **Step 1** — Glucose → Glucose-6-Phosphate, catalyzed by **Hexokinase** (or Glucokinase in liver).
2. **Step 3** — Fructose-6-Phosphate → Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphate, catalyzed by **Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)** — the major rate-limiting enzyme, allosterically activated by AMP/ADP/F2,6BP and inhibited by ATP/citrate.
3. **Step 10** — Phosphoenolpyruvate → Pyruvate, catalyzed by **Pyruvate Kinase (PK)**, activated by F1,6BP (feed-forward activation).

These steps govern the **overall flux** of the pathway and are key targets for hormonal regulation.

---

### **PART B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation from fundamental electrochemical potential principles. Compute the equilibrium potential for $K^+$ at 37 °C given $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_o = 4.5$ mM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mammalian neuron at rest has the following intracellular and extracellular concentrations (in mM): $K^+$: 140 / 5; $Na^+$: 15 / 145; $Cl^-$: 4 / 120. Using the GHK voltage equation with permeability ratios $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.4$, calculate the resting membrane potential at body temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation steps are as in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Show the electrochemical potential, apply equilibrium, solve for $E_X$, and convert to log base 10.

Numerical evaluation:

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\frac{4.5}{140} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03214)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.03214) = -1.493$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.493) = -91.8\ mV$$

> **Answer:** $E_K \approx -92$ mV (consistent with experimental −90 mV).

**(b)** GHK Equation:

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

**Numerator:** $1(5) + 0.04(145) + 0.4(4) = 5 + 5.8 + 1.6 = 12.4$

**Denominator:** $1(140) + 0.04(15) + 0.4(120) = 140 + 0.6 + 48 = 188.6$

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\frac{12.4}{188.6} = 61.5 \log_{10}(0.06575)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.06575) = -1.1822$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1822) = -72.7\ mV$$

> **Answer:** $V_m \approx -72.7$ mV (matches measured resting potential).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Write the Michaelis–Menten rate law for a single-enzyme reaction. Using the **steady-state approximation**, derive the expression for $v_0$ in terms of $[E]_T$, $[S]$, $k_{cat}$, and $K_m$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) catalyzed step in glycolysis, the following data are obtained at fixed enzyme concentration: $V_{max} = 1.2$ mM·s⁻¹, $K_m = 0.15$ mM. Determine the substrate concentration required to achieve 75% of $V_{max}$. Comment on the regulatory significance of PFK-1 in glycolysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Michaelis–Menten Derivation — as in **Section 3.5**.

- Reaction scheme: $E + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} ES \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} E + P$
- Mass balance: $[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$
- Steady state: $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow [ES] = \dfrac{[E]_T[S]}{K_m + [S]}$
- Rate: $v_0 = k_{cat}[ES] = \dfrac{k_{cat}[E]_T[S]}{K_m + [S]} = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$
- Where $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$, $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1}+k_{cat}}{k_1}$

**(b)** Set $v_0 = 0.75\,V_{max}$:

$$0.75\,V_{max} = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

$$0.75(K_m + [S]) = [S]$$

$$0.75\,K_m = 0.25\,[S]$$

$$[S] = 3\,K_m = 3 \times 0.15 = 0.45\ mM$$

> **Answer:** $[S] = 0.45$ mM is required for 75% saturation.

**Regulatory significance of PFK-1:**
PFK-1 catalyzes the **committed, rate-limiting step** of glycolysis (F6P → F1,6BP). It is allosterically activated by **AMP, ADP, and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (F2,6BP)** — signals of low energy and high glycolytic demand. It is allosterically inhibited by **ATP and citrate** — signals of high energy and abundant alternative fuels. This makes PFK-1 the **master regulatory switch** of glycolytic flux and a primary focus of metabolic control analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong sign of Nernst potential:** Always define $E_X$ as **inside minus outside** ($V_i - V_o$). For ions with $z>0$ and $[X]_i > [X]_o$ (like K⁺), $E_X$ is **negative**.
> 2. **Inverted chloride ratio in GHK:** $[Cl^-]_i$ goes in the **numerator** and $[Cl^-]_o$ in the **denominator** because $z_{Cl} = -1$.
> 3. **PFK-1 vs Pyruvate Kinase confusion:** PFK-1 catalyzes step 3 (F6P → F1,6BP); Pyruvate Kinase catalyzes step 10 (PEP → Pyruvate).
> 4. **Membrane potential ≠ Action potential:** Resting potential is the steady value; action potential is the transient spike involving Na⁺/K⁺ gating.
> 5. **Show all steps:** KTU examiners award partial marks for each derivation step. Skipping algebraic transitions costs 1–2 marks per question.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- **Glycolysis overall equation:** Glucose + 2 NAD⁺ + 2 ADP + 2 Pᵢ → 2 Pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H₂O.
- **Net ATP yield (anaerobic glycolysis):** **2 ATP** per glucose (subtracting the 2 ATP investment).
- **Site of glycolysis:** **Cytosol** of all living cells — does not require oxygen or mitochondria.
- **Three regulatory enzymes:** **Hexokinase**, **Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)**, and **Pyruvate Kinase**.
- **PFK-1 activator:** **F2,6BP** (most potent); **AMP/ADP**.
- **PFK-1 inhibitor:** **ATP, Citrate, H⁺ (Pasteur effect)**.
- **Michaelis–Menten form:** $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$; $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$.
- **Nernst Equation:** $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ mV (at 37 °C).
- **GHK Voltage:** $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$.
- **Nernst values (mammalian neuron, 37 °C):** $E_K \approx -90$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV, $E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV.
- **Resting potential:** ~ **−70 mV** (inside negative), set mainly by K⁺ permeability.
- **Action potential phases:** Resting → **Depolarization (Na⁺ in, m-gate opens)** → **Peak (+30 mV)** → **Repolarization (K⁺ out, n-gate opens)** → **Hyperpolarization (K⁺ still open)** → **Restoration (Na⁺/K⁺ pump).**
- **Hodgkin–Huxley gating:** $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\,m^3 h\,(V-E_{Na})$; $I_K = \bar{g}_K\,n^4(V-E_K)$; $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V)x$.
- **Cable equation:** $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{r_m c_m}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{V}{r_m}$ — governs spatial propagation.
- **Constant to memorize:** $2.303\,RT/F = 61.5$ mV at 37 °C.
- **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Expect numerical problems combining the **Nernst** and **GHK** equations with given ion concentrations and permeability ratios — practice both directions (find $E_X$ given concentrations, or find $V_m$ given all permeabilities).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution of Partial Differential Equations: Elliptic, Parabolic, Hyperbolic Equations. Applications: Dynamics of the Basilar Membrane, Drug Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT744 - COMPUTATIONAL METHODS TO BIOMEDICAL PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solution of Partial Differential Equations: Elliptic, Parabolic, Hyperbolic Equations. Applications: Dynamics of the Basilar Membrane, Drug Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Partial Differential Equation (PDE)?

A **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** is a mathematical equation that involves an unknown function of **multiple independent variables** and its partial derivatives with respect to those variables. In biomedical engineering, PDEs are the language in which nature describes the spatial and temporal evolution of physical, chemical, and biological fields such as drug concentration, electrical potential, mechanical displacement, and pressure.

A general second-order linear PDE in two independent variables is written as:

$$A(x,y) \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + 2B(x,y) \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial y} + C(x,y) \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + D(x,y) \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + E(x,y) \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + F(x,y) u = G(x,y)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every second-order PDE you will encounter in this module can be classified into exactly **three** canonical families: **Elliptic**, **Parabolic**, or **Hyperbolic**. The classifier is the discriminant $D = B^2 - AC$.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical PDE Families (KTU Board Definition)

| Type | Discriminant | Canonical Equation | Prototype Biomedical Phenomenon |
|------|--------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Elliptic** | $B^2 - AC < 0$ | $\nabla^2 u = f(x,y)$ (Poisson/Laplace) | Steady-state electric potential in the heart (ECG) |
| **Parabolic** | $B^2 - AC = 0$ | $u_t = c^2 \, u_{xx}$ (Heat/Diffusion) | Drug diffusion through skin/tissue |
| **Hyperbolic** | $B^2 - AC > 0$ | $u_{tt} = c^2 \, u_{xx}$ (Wave) | Basilar membrane vibration in the cochlea |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Equation 1 — Elliptic (Steady-State):**
