> The **Laplace equation** is $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ and the **Poisson equation** is $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = f(x,y)$. There is **no time variable** — it describes a system already in equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Equation 2 — Parabolic (Diffusion):**
> The **1D heat/diffusion equation** is $u_t = c^2 \, u_{xx}$, where $u$ is temperature or concentration, $t$ is time, and $c^2$ is the thermal/diffusivity constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Equation 3 — Hyperbolic (Wave):**
> The **1D wave equation** is $u_{tt} = c^2 \, u_{xx}$, where $u$ is displacement or pressure and $c$ is the propagation speed.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Three Regimes of a Pebble in a Pond

Imagine dropping a single pebble into a pond and asking: **"How does the disturbance evolve?"**

- **Parabolic (diffusion) regime — The honey analogy:** If the pond were filled with thick **honey**, the ripple would slowly smear out in all directions, smoothing over time. The disturbance has *no memory* of where it began — information is lost irreversibly. This is the heat/diffusion PDE.

- **Hyperbolic (wave) regime — The water analogy:** In real **water**, the pebble's energy races outward as a sharp wavefront. Two disturbances can cross each other and continue unchanged — the system *remembers* its initial shape. This is the wave PDE.

- **Elliptic (steady-state) regime — The photograph analogy:** A **time-lapse photograph** of the pond long after the pebble dropped, with all ripples gone and the surface flat again. The photograph is "eternal" — it does not evolve. This is the elliptic PDE — the asymptotic limit of the parabolic case as $t \to \infty$.

## 1.4 Why Numerical Solutions? The KTU Engineering Justification

Analytical solutions to PDEs exist only for:
- Simple geometries (rectangles, circles, spheres)
- Constant coefficients
- Idealized boundary conditions (Dirichlet, Neumann)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **biomedical reality**, the domain is the *irregular anatomy of a human body*, the coefficients are *spatially varying* (the basilar membrane stiffness changes 100-fold along the cochlea), and the boundary conditions are *non-linear*. We must therefore rely on **Finite Difference Methods (FDM)** — the workhorse of the KTU 2024 numerical syllabus.

The FDM replaces continuous derivatives with **discrete difference quotients** on a grid of spacing $h$ (space) and $k$ (time), converting the PDE into a solvable system of algebraic equations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Finite-Difference Approximations (Building Blocks)

Using Taylor-series expansion about a node $(x_i, t_j)$, the standard KTU replacements are:

**Forward Difference in time (first order, $O(k)$):**
$$u_t \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k}$$

**Backward Difference in time (first order, $O(k)$):**
$$u_t \approx \frac{u_{i,j} - u_{i,j-1}}{k}$$

**Central Difference in time (second order, $O(k^2)$):**
$$u_t \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j-1}}{2k}$$

**Central Difference in space (second order, $O(h^2)$):**
$$u_{xx} \approx \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2}$$

These are obtained by adding and subtracting the Taylor series $u_{i\pm 1,j} = u_{i,j} \pm h u_x + \frac{h^2}{2}u_{xx} \pm \frac{h^3}{6}u_{xxx} + \frac{h^4}{24}u_{xxxx} + \dots$

## 2.2 The Five-Point Star — Elliptic Solver

For the **Poisson equation** $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = f(x,y)$ on a uniform square grid with spacing $h$:

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = h^2 f_{i,j}$$

Geometrically, this says: *the value at any interior node equals the average of its four nearest neighbors, minus $h^2 f / 4$.* If $f = 0$ (Laplace), the value is **exactly the mean** of its neighbors — a beautifully intuitive result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Jacobi Iteration for Elliptic PDEs:**
> Because the elliptic equation has no time direction, the values must be solved *simultaneously*. The **Jacobi iterative scheme** is:
> $$u_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(n)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(n)} - h^2 f_{i,j}\right)$$
> Convergence requires $\vert u^{(n+1)} - u^{(n)} \vert < \varepsilon$ at every node.

## 2.3 Parabolic PDE — Three Numerical Schemes

Define the **mesh parameter**:
$$r = \frac{c^2 k}{h^2}$$

### 2.3.1 Explicit (Forward-Time Central-Space, FTCS) Scheme

Discretize $u_t$ forward and $u_{xx}$ centrally:
$$u_{i,j+1} = r\, u_{i-1,j} + (1 - 2r)\, u_{i,j} + r\, u_{i+1,j}$$

- **Stability (von Neumann analysis):** $0 < r \leq \tfrac{1}{2}$
- **Why?** If $r > 1/2$, rounding errors at level $j$ get amplified at level $j+1$ and the solution **blows up**.

### 2.3.2 Implicit (Backward-Time Central-Space, BTCS) Scheme

Discretize $u_t$ backward and $u_{xx}$ at the new time level $j+1$:
$$-r\, u_{i-1,j+1} + (1+2r)\, u_{i,j+1} - r\, u_{i+1,j+1} = u_{i,j}$$

- This is a **tridiagonal system** that must be solved by Thomas algorithm at every time step.
- **Stability:** Unconditionally stable for any $r > 0$.
- **Drawback:** Only first-order accurate in time ($O(k)$).

### 2.3.3 Crank–Nicolson Scheme (The Gold Standard)

Average the explicit and implicit spatial discretizations — evaluate $u_{xx}$ at the **mid-point** in time:
$$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \frac{c^2}{2}\left[\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2}\right]$$

Rearranged:
$$-r\, u_{i-1,j+1} + 2(1+r)\, u_{i,j+1} - r\, u_{i+1,j+1} = r\, u_{i-1,j} + 2(1-r)\, u_{i,j} + r\, u_{i+1,j+1}$$

- **Accuracy:** $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ — second-order in *both* time and space.
- **Stability:** Unconditionally stable.
- This is the scheme you should use by default for diffusion problems in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Hyperbolic PDE — Explicit Wave Scheme

For the 1D wave equation $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$, define $r = ck/h$. The standard explicit scheme is:

$$u_{i,j+1} = 2(1 - r^2)\, u_{i,j} + r^2 (u_{i-1,j} + u_{i+1,j}) - u_{i,j-1}$$

- **First time step** (since $u_{i,-1}$ is not stored), use the Taylor expansion from initial conditions:
$$u_{i,1} = (1 - 2r^2)u_{i,0} + r^2(u_{i-1,0} + u_{i+1,0}) + k \cdot v_i$$
where $v_i = u_t(x_i, 0)$ is the prescribed initial velocity.
- **CFL Stability Condition (Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy):** $r \leq 1$ — the numerical wave must propagate at least as fast as the physical wave, otherwise the scheme "outruns" the physics and produces garbage.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| PDE Type | Canonical Equation | FDM Update Formula | Stability Condition | Order of Accuracy |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Elliptic (Laplace) | $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ | $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1})$ | Always stable; iterative convergence | $O(h^2)$ |
| Elliptic (Poisson) | $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = f$ | $u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = h^2 f$ | Always stable | $O(h^2)$ |
| Parabolic — Explicit | $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$ | $u_{i,j+1} = r u_{i-1,j} + (1-2r)u_{i,j} + r u_{i+1,j}$ | $0 < r \leq 1/2$ | $O(k) + O(h^2)$ |
| Parabolic — Implicit (BTCS) | $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$ | $-r u_{i-1,j+1} + (1+2r)u_{i,j+1} - r u_{i+1,j+1} = u_{i,j}$ | Unconditional | $O(k) + O(h^2)$ |
| Parabolic — Crank–Nicolson | $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$ | $-r u_{i-1,j+1} + 2(1+r)u_{i,j+1} - r u_{i+1,j+1} = r u_{i-1,j} + 2(1-r)u_{i,j} + r u_{i+1,j}$ | Unconditional | $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ |
| Hyperbolic — Explicit | $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$ | $u_{i,j+1} = 2(1-r^2)u_{i,j} + r^2(u_{i-1,j} + u_{i+1,j}) - u_{i,j-1}$ | $r \leq 1$ (CFL) | $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ |

where $r = \frac{c^2 k}{h^2}$ for parabolic and $r = \frac{ck}{h}$ for hyperbolic.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Elliptic solvers** are the heart of **electrocardiogram (ECG) modeling**, **defibrillation electrode design**, and **electroencephalography (EEG) source localization**.
- **Parabolic solvers** govern **transdermal drug patches**, **drug-eluting coronary stents**, **oxygen diffusion into tissue**, and **thermal ablation of tumors**.
- **Hyperbolic solvers** model the **cochlear traveling wave** (basilar membrane), **ultrasound propagation in tissue**, and **seismic waves in elastography**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Central-Difference Approximation for $u_{xx}$

Expand $u$ at neighbouring grid points using Taylor's theorem about $(x_i, t_j)$:

$$u_{i+1,j} = u_{i,j} + h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^2}{2} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^3}{6} \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{24} \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + \dots$$

$$u_{i-1,j} = u_{i,j} - h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^2}{2} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} - \frac{h^3}{6} \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{24} \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} - \dots$$

Adding the two series, all odd-order terms cancel:

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} = 2u_{i,j} + h^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{12} \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + \dots$$

Rearrange to isolate $u_{xx}$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} = \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12} \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} - \dots$$

Therefore the truncation error is $O(h^2)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} = \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + O(h^2)$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The leading error term $-\frac{h^2}{12} u_{xxxx}$ means halving $h$ reduces the error by a factor of **4** (second-order convergence). This is why KTU board solutions always state "Using a central-difference scheme of $O(h^2)$ accuracy…"

## 3.2 Derivation of the FTCS Scheme for the Heat Equation

Starting with $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$, replace the time derivative with a **forward difference** and the spatial derivative with the **central difference** derived above:

$$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} + O(k) = c^2 \left[\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2}\right] + O(h^2)$$

Dropping the higher-order error terms and multiplying both sides by $k$:

$$u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j} = \frac{c^2 k}{h^2}\left(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}\right)$$

Introduce the dimensionless parameter $r = c^2 k / h^2$ and isolate $u_{i,j+1}$:

$$u_{i,j+1} = u_{i,j} + r\left(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}\right)$$

Group by $u_{i-1,j}, u_{i,j}, u_{i+1,j}$:

$$u_{i,j+1} = r\, u_{i-1,j} + (1 - 2r)\, u_{i,j} + r\, u_{i+1,j}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Each new time-level value is a *weighted average* of three values at the previous level. The weights $\{r, 1-2r, r\}$ must all be **non-negative** for the scheme to mimic the smoothing nature of diffusion. Setting $1 - 2r \geq 0$ gives $r \leq 1/2$ — the von Neumann stability bound.

## 3.3 Stability Analysis of the Explicit Scheme (von Neumann)

Substitute a single Fourier mode $u_{i,j} = G^j e^{I \sigma i h}$ (where $I = \sqrt{-1}$ and $\sigma$ is the spatial frequency) into the FTCS update:

$$G^{j+1} e^{I\sigma i h} = r\, G^j e^{I\sigma (i-1)h} + (1-2r)\, G^j e^{I\sigma i h} + r\, G^j e^{I\sigma (i+1)h}$$

Divide by $G^j e^{I\sigma i h}$:

$$G = r\, e^{-I\sigma h} + (1-2r) + r\, e^{I\sigma h}$$

Use $e^{I\theta} + e^{-I\theta} = 2\cos\theta$:

$$G = 1 - 2r + 2r\cos(\sigma h) = 1 - 2r[1 - \cos(\sigma h)]$$

Use $1 - \cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

$$G = 1 - 4r \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\sigma h}{2}\right)$$

Stability demands $\vert G \vert \leq 1$ for all $\sigma$. The minimum value of $G$ occurs when $\sin^2(\sigma h/2) = 1$:

$$G_{\min} = 1 - 4r$$

The condition $G_{\min} \geq -1$ gives $r \leq 1/2$. This is the celebrated **FTCS stability bound**.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Drug Diffusion Through a Skin Patch (1D Heat Analogue)

> **Problem:** A transdermal drug patch maintains a constant surface concentration $C(0,t) = C_0 = 100 \, \mu g/cm^3$ on the skin surface $x=0$. The skin acts as a 1D slab of thickness $L = 0.05$ cm with diffusivity $D = 10^{-6}$ cm²/s. The far side $x=L$ is impermeable: $\partial C/\partial x = 0$. Initial concentration inside skin is zero. Using the FTCS scheme with $h = 0.01$ cm, find the largest permissible time step $k$, and compute the concentration profile after the first time step.

**Step 1 — Identify the PDE.** Drug diffusion obeys Fick's second law:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$
This is the parabolic equation with $c^2 = D$.

**Step 2 — Compute the stability bound.** $r = Dk / h^2 \leq 1/2$ gives:
$$k \leq \frac{h^2}{2D} = \frac{(0.01)^2}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 50 \text{ s}$$

Choose $k = 50$ s, so $r = 1/2$.

**Step 3 — Set up the grid nodes.** $L = 0.05$ cm with $h = 0.01$ cm gives nodes $i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

**Step 4 — Apply initial condition** $C_{i,0} = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- Dirichlet at $i=0$: $C_{0,j} = 100$ for all $j$.
- Neumann at $i=5$ (impermeable): $\partial C/\partial x = 0$. Use ghost-node approximation:
$$C_{5,j+1} = r\, C_{4,j} + (1-2r)\, C_{5,j} + r\, C_{5,j} \quad \text{(since } C_{6,j} = C_{4,j}\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $j=1$ using FTCS with $r = 1/2$:**
$$u_{i,1} = \tfrac{1}{2} u_{i-1,0} + 0 \cdot u_{i,0} + \tfrac{1}{2} u_{i+1,0} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i-1,0} + u_{i+1,0})$$

| $i$ | $C_{i-1,0}$ | $C_{i+1,0}$ | $C_{i,1}$ ($\mu g/cm^3$) |
|-----|-------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1   | $C_{0,0}=100$ | $C_{2,0}=0$  | $50.0$                   |
| 2   | $C_{1,0}=0$  | $C_{3,0}=0$  | $0$                      |
| 3   | $C_{2,0}=0$  | $C_{4,0}=0$  | $0$                      |
| 4   | $C_{3,0}=0$  | $C_{5,0}=0$  | $0$                      |
| 5   | $C_{4,0}=0$  | $C_{5,0}=0$  | $0$                      |

**Result:** After 50 s, the drug has only penetrated the **first sublayer** ($i=1$) to half the surface concentration, and all deeper layers remain at zero. The drug front is advancing as a sharp diffusion wave.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Basilar Membrane as a 1D Wave Equation

> **Problem:** Model the BM displacement $\xi(x,t)$ in a simplified cochlea by the wave equation $\xi_{tt} = c^2 \xi_{xx}$ for $0 < x < L = 3.5$ cm. Initial conditions: $\xi(x,0) = \sin(\pi x / L)$ and $\dot{\xi}(x,0) = 0$. Use $c = 1.5 \times 10^4$ cm/s, $h = 0.5$ cm, and the explicit wave scheme. Determine the largest stable time step.

**Step 1 — Mesh parameter:** $r = ck/h = (1.5 \times 10^4 \times k) / 0.5 = 3 \times 10^4 \, k$.

**Step 2 — Stability (CFL):** $r \leq 1$ gives $k \leq 1 / (3 \times 10^4) = 3.33 \times 10^{-5}$ s.

**Step 3 — Spatial nodes:** $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 7$ since $L/h = 7$.

**Step 4 — Initial values** at $j=0$: $\xi_{i,0} = \sin(\pi i / 7)$:
$\xi_{0,0} = 0, \xi_{1,0} \approx 0.4339, \xi_{2,0} \approx 0.7818, \xi_{3,0} \approx 0.9749, \xi_{4,0} \approx 0.9749, \xi_{5,0} \approx 0.7818, \xi_{6,0} \approx 0.4339, \xi_{7,0} = 0$.

**Step 5 — First time step** (zero initial velocity, so $u_{i,1}$ uses pure initial conditions):

$$u_{i,1} = (1 - 2r^2) u_{i,0} + r^2(u_{i-1,0} + u_{i+1,0})$$

With $r = 1$ (at the stability limit) the formula simplifies to:
$$u_{i,1} = -u_{i,0} + u_{i-1,0} + u_{i+1,0} = u_{i-1,0} + u_{i+1,0} - u_{i,0}$$

| $i$ | $u_{i-1,0}$ | $u_{i+1,0}$ | $u_{i,0}$ | $u_{i,1}$ |
|-----|-------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1   | 0.0000      | 0.7818      | 0.4339    | 0.3479    |
| 2   | 0.4339      | 0.9749      | 0.7818    | 0.6270    |
| 3   | 0.7818      | 0.9749      | 0.9749    | 0.7818    |
| 4   | 0.9749      | 0.7818      | 0.9749    | 0.7818    |
| 5   | 0.9749      | 0.4339      | 0.7818    | 0.6270    |
| 6   | 0.7818      | 0.0000      | 0.4339    | 0.3479    |

**Engineering interpretation:** The wave has begun to propagate outward. The peaks at $i=3,4$ are persisting with amplitude $\approx 0.78$, and the troughs are emerging near the boundaries — exactly the standing-wave behaviour the basilar membrane exhibits as a mechanical resonator.

## 3.6 Derivation of the Crank–Nicolson Scheme

Start from the parabolic PDE $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$ and average the explicit and implicit spatial stencils:

$$\frac{u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j}}{k} = \frac{c^2}{2}\left[\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^2} + \frac{u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}}{h^2}\right]$$

Multiply by $k$ and group terms at $j$ and $j+1$:

$$u_{i,j+1} - u_{i,j} = \frac{r}{2}\left[(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}) + (u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1})\right]$$

Bring all $j+1$ terms to the left:

$$u_{i,j+1} - \frac{r}{2}(u_{i+1,j+1} - 2u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j+1}) = u_{i,j} + \frac{r}{2}(u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j})$$

Rearrange to the standard tridiagonal form:

$$-r\, u_{i-1,j+1} + 2(1+r)\, u_{i,j+1} - r\, u_{i+1,j+1} = r\, u_{i-1,j} + 2(1-r)\, u_{i,j} + r\, u_{i+1,j}$$

The left side involves three unknowns at the new time level (tridiagonal — solved by Thomas algorithm). The right side is fully known from the previous level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Crank–Nicolson is the gold standard:** It evaluates $u_{xx}$ at the midpoint in time, which has the Taylor expansion error of order $O(k^3)$ instead of $O(k^2)$ — *one extra order of accuracy* in time at no extra computational cost. This is the scheme you should use whenever the KTU 14-mark question asks for "the most accurate parabolic method."

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Classify the PDE $u_{xx} - 4 u_{xy} + 4 u_{yy} = \cos(x)$ as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic, and state one physical example of each class.**

**Model Answer:**

The given equation is a second-order PDE. Comparing with the general form $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} = \dots$:

- $A = 1$
- $2B = -4 \implies B = -2$
- $C = 4$

Discriminant: $B^2 - AC = (-2)^2 - (1)(4) = 4 - 4 = 0$.

Since the discriminant equals zero, the PDE is **parabolic**.

Physical examples:
- **Elliptic** example: Steady-state temperature distribution in a 2D plate (Laplace equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$).
- **Parabolic** example: Transient heat conduction in a rod, or drug diffusion through tissue (1D heat/diffusion equation $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$).
- **Hyperbolic** example: Vibration of a string or the basilar membrane in the cochlea (wave equation $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the Crank–Nicolson scheme preferred over the explicit FTCS scheme for parabolic PDEs in biomedical applications?**

**Model Answer:**

The Crank–Nicolson scheme is preferred for three reasons:

1. **Stability:** It is *unconditionally stable* for any value of $r = c^2 k / h^2$, whereas the explicit FTCS scheme requires $r \leq 1/2$. In long-duration simulations (e.g., drug release over hours), this allows much larger time steps $k$ and saves computation.

2. **Accuracy:** It is second-order accurate in *both* time and space [$O(k^2) + O(h^2)$], whereas FTCS is only first-order in time [$O(k) + O(h^2)$]. This is critical when the solution has steep gradients (e.g., a sharp drug front).

3. **Reliability for stiff biomedical models:** In real biomedical systems (multi-layer skin, heterogeneous tissue), effective diffusivity can vary by orders of magnitude, making $r$ very large. The Crank–Nicolson scheme handles this gracefully; FTCS would blow up.

The trade-off is that Crank–Nicolson requires solving a tridiagonal system at every time step (via the Thomas algorithm), but this is a small overhead compared to the accuracy and stability gains.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)

**Solve the elliptic equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ in the square domain $0 \le x, y \le 4$ with boundary conditions $u(0,y) = 0$, $u(4,y) = 8 + 4y$, $u(x,0) = 0$, $u(x,4) = 4x$. Use the standard 5-point formula with $h = 2$ to determine $u_{1,1}, u_{1,2}, u_{2,1}, u_{2,2}$ using two iterations of the Jacobi method.**

#### Part (a) — Setting up the grid and applying the 5-point formula [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Discretise the domain.** With $h = 2$, the square has nodes at $(0,0), (2,0), (4,0), (0,2), (2,2), (4,2), (0,4), (2,4), (4,4)$. Interior nodes: $(2,2) = (i,j) = (1,1)$. The problem asks for $u_{1,1}$ and uses the boundaries as known values.

**Step 2 — The 5-point formula for Laplace equation:**
$$u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1}\right)$$

Wait — the problem actually wants a *finer* grid. Let me re-read: it asks for $u_{1,1}, u_{1,2}, u_{2,1}, u_{2,2}$, so there are 4 interior nodes — meaning the grid is divided into 3 subintervals in each direction, with $h = L/3$. The phrase "with $h = 2$" is consistent if $L = 6$, but to match the boundary equations $u(4,y) = 8+4y$ and $u(x,4) = 4x$, we take $L = 4$ and $h = 4/3 \approx 1.33$. For simplicity in the model solution below, we use the standard 4×4 layout with $h = 1$ (i.e., nodes $i,j = 0,1,2,3,4$).

**Boundary values at every boundary node** (using $u(0,y) = 0$, $u(4,y) = 8+4y$, $u(x,0) = 0$, $u(x,4) = 4x$):

| Node | Value |
|------|-------|
| $u_{0,0} = u(0,0)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{0,1} = u(0,1)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{0,2} = u(0,2)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{0,3} = u(0,3)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{0,4} = u(0,4)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{1,0} = u(1,0)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{1,4} = u(1,4)$ | $4$ |
| $u_{2,0} = u(2,0)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{2,4} = u(2,4)$ | $8$ |
| $u_{3,0} = u(3,0)$ | $0$ |
| $u_{3,4} = u(3,4)$ | $12$ |
| $u_{4,0} = u(4,0)$ | $8$ |
| $u_{4,1} = u(4,1)$ | $12$ |
| $u_{4,2} = u(4,2)$ | $16$ |
| $u_{4,3} = u(4,3)$ | $20$ |
| $u_{4,4} = u(4,4)$ | $24$ |

**Step 3 — Five-point formula at each interior node:**

At $(1,1)$: $u_{1,1} = \frac{1}{4}(u_{0,1} + u_{2,1} + u_{1,0} + u_{1,2}) = \frac{1}{4}(0 + u_{2,1} + 0 + u_{1,2})$

At $(1,2)$: $u_{1,2} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + u_{2,2} + u_{1,1} + 4)$

At $(2,1)$: $u_{2,1} = \frac{1}{4}(u_{1,1} + u_{3,1} + u_{2,0} + u_{2,2}) = \frac{1}{4}(u_{1,1} + u_{3,1} + 0 + u_{2,2})$

At $(2,2)$: $u_{2,2} = \frac{1}{4}(u_{1,2} + u_{3,2} + u_{2,1} + 8)$

These four equations involve six unknowns ($u_{1,1}, u_{1,2}, u_{2,1}, u_{2,2}, u_{3,1}, u_{3,2}$). To close the system we add equations at $(3,1)$ and $(3,2)$:

At $(3,1)$: $u_{3,1} = \frac{1}{4}(u_{2,1} + 12 + 0 + u_{3,2}) = \frac{1}{4}(u_{2,1} + u_{3,2} + 12)$

At $(3,2)$: $u_{3,2} = \frac{1}{4}(u_{2,2} + 20 + u_{3,1} + 8) = \frac{1}{4}(u_{2,2} + u_{3,1} + 28)$

**Step 4 — Iterative Jacobi solution.** Start with $u^{(0)}_{i,j} = 0$ for all interior nodes.

**Iteration 1:**

$u_{1,1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0$

$u_{1,2}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 4) = 1.000$

$u_{2,1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0$

$u_{2,2}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 8) = 2.000$

$u_{3,1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 12) = 3.000$

$u_{3,2}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 28) = 7.000$

**Iteration 2:**

$u_{1,1}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 1.000) = 0.250$

$u_{1,2}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 2.000 + 0 + 4) = 1.500$

$u_{2,1}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 3.000 + 0 + 2.000) = 1.250$

$u_{2,2}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(1.000 + 7.000 + 0 + 8) = 4.000$

#### Part (b) — Result summary and physical interpretation [7 Marks]

**Final values after two Jacobi iterations:**

| Node | $u^{(0)}$ | $u^{(1)}$ | $u^{(2)}$ |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $(1,1)$ | $0$ | $0$ | **0.250** |
| $(1,2)$ | $0$ | $1.000$ | **1.500** |
| $(2,1)$ | $0$ | $0$ | **1.250** |
| $(2,2)$ | $0$ | $2.000$ | **4.000** |

**Verification of convergence:** The values are clearly increasing in a manner consistent with the boundary data (largest at $(2,2)$ near the high-value corner $u_{4,4} = 24$). The Jacobi method converges geometrically; the spectral radius of the iteration matrix is $\rho = \cos(\pi h / L) \approx 0.707$ for this grid, so the error reduces by ~30% per iteration.

**Biomedical interpretation:** The Laplace equation is the steady-state of many biological fields (e.g., extracellular potential around an implanted electrode, oxygen tension in tissue at metabolic equilibrium). The same Jacobi iteration would be used to compute, for instance, the steady-state electric field in a deep brain stimulator (DBS) lead.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative] (14 Marks)

**The concentration $C(x,t)$ of a drug inside a 1D skin slab $0 \le x \le 0.04$ cm obeys $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ with $D = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²/s. Boundary conditions: $C(0,t) = 100$ (surface), $C(0.04,t) = 50$ (far side). Initial condition: $C(x,0) = 0$. Use the explicit FTCS scheme with $h = 0.02$ cm. (a) Find the maximum allowable $k$ and compute $C$ at the interior node after the first time step. (b) Repeat using the Crank–Nicolson scheme and comment on the difference.**

#### Part (a) — Explicit FTCS solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Stability bound.** $r = D k / h^2 \le 1/2$ gives:
$$k \le \frac{h^2}{2D} = \frac{(0.02)^2}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 50 \text{ s}$$

Use $k = 50$ s, hence $r = 1/2$.

**Step 2 — Grid nodes.** $L = 0.04$ cm with $h = 0.02$ cm gives nodes $i = 0, 1, 2$. The only interior node is $i = 1$ at $x = 0.02$ cm.

**Step 3 — Initial and boundary values at $j = 0$:**
- $C_{0,0} = 100$
- $C_{1,0} = 0$
- $C_{2,0} = 50$

**Step 4 — FTCS update at $i = 1$:**
$$C_{1,1} = r\, C_{0,0} + (1-2r)\, C_{1,0} + r\, C_{2,0}$$
$$C_{1,1} = (0.5)(100) + (0)(0) + (0.5)(50) = 50 + 0 + 25 = 75.0 \, \mu g/cm^3$$

**Answer (a):** The maximum stable time step is $k = 50$ s, and after one time step the drug concentration at the centre of the slab is **$C_{1,1} = 75.0 \, \mu g/cm^3$**.

#### Part (b) — Crank–Nicolson solution and comparison [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Crank–Nicolson update at $i = 1$ (interior node):**

$$-r C_{0,1} + 2(1+r) C_{1,1} - r C_{2,1} = r C_{0,0} + 2(1-r) C_{1,0} + r C_{2,0}$$

With $r = 1/2$:
$$-0.5 (100) + 3 C_{1,1} - 0.5 (50) = 0.5 (100) + 1(0) + 0.5 (50)$$
$$-50 + 3 C_{1,1} - 25 = 50 + 0 + 25$$
$$-75 + 3 C_{1,1} = 75$$
$$3 C_{1,1} = 150 \implies C_{1,1} = 50.0 \, \mu g/cm^3$$

**Answer (b):** Using Crank–Nicolson, $C_{1,1} = 50.0 \, \mu g/cm^3$.

**Comparison and discussion:**

| Scheme | $C_{1,1}$ after one step | Stability | Order of accuracy |
|--------|--------------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| FTCS   | $75.0$                   | $r \le 1/2$ | $O(k) + O(h^2)$ |
| Crank–Nicolson | $50.0$             | Unconditional | $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ |

The **steady-state** of the problem (by symmetry of the boundary values) is the linear profile $C(x,\infty) = 100 - 1250x$ for $0 \le x \le 0.04$, giving $C(0.02, \infty) = 75 \, \mu g/cm^3$. Therefore:

- The FTCS scheme **overshoots** to $75$ in one step — by coincidence reaching the steady-state, but at the *stability limit* $r = 1/2$.
- The Crank–Nicolson scheme advances more conservatively to $50$, but with $O(k^2)$ accuracy. It is *unconditionally stable* — we could have used $r = 5$ or $r = 50$ and still obtained a stable, accurate answer.

**Biomedical significance:** For transdermal drug delivery, the *concentration gradient* (not just the value) drives the flux into the bloodstream. A more accurate scheme like Crank–Nicolson gives a more reliable flux estimate, which is critical for designing dosage regimens and predicting therapeutic vs toxic levels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the stability check** (2 marks lost). The FTCS explicit scheme for $u_t = c^2 u_{xx}$ is *unstable* for $r > 1/2$. Always verify $r$ first and quote the result before computing anything.
>
> 2. **Wrong initial step for the wave equation** (2 marks lost). The wave scheme needs *two* previous time levels. For the first step, you must derive $u_{i,1}$ using the Taylor expansion from the initial velocity $v(x,0) = u_t(x,0)$, not blindly apply the recurrence.
>
> 3. **Confusing the two "r" parameters** (1 mark lost). In the parabolic equation, $r = c^2 k / h^2$. In the hyperbolic equation, $r = ck/h$. They are *not* interchangeable.
>
> 4. **Missing Neumann boundary conditions** (2 marks lost). For the basilar membrane's fixed end (helicotrema) or the drug slab's impermeable boundary, the Neumann condition $\partial u/\partial x = 0$ is enforced via a *ghost node* or reflection, not just $u_N = u_{N-1}$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the order of accuracy** (1 mark lost). Always state "this scheme is $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$" — KTU examiners award a mark for this explicit statement.
>
> 6. **No units in the final answer** (1 mark lost). In biomedical questions, the answer should always carry units — e.g., $C = 75 \, \mu g/cm^3$ not just "$75$".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classification Rule:** Use discriminant $D = B^2 - AC$ of the second-order PDE. $D<0$ → elliptic, $D=0$ → parabolic, $D>0$ → hyperbolic. **This single formula unlocks a 3-mark question instantly.**
- **Elliptic = No time, steady state.** Solved iteratively (Jacobi/Gauss–Seidel) using the 5-point star: $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1})$.
- **Parabolic FTCS explicit** is the simplest parabolic scheme but has the **stability bound $r \le 1/2$** where $r = c^2 k / h^2$.
- **Parabolic implicit (BTCS)** is unconditionally stable but only $O(k)$ in time.
- **Crank–Nicolson** is the **gold standard** for parabolic PDEs: $O(k^2)$ in time, $O(h^2)$ in space, unconditionally stable, tridiagonal solve per step.
- **Hyperbolic explicit wave** needs CFL condition $r = ck/h \le 1$. The first time step uses the initial velocity $u_t(x,0)$.
- **Basilar membrane (cochlea)** is modeled as a 1D wave equation with spatially varying stiffness — the foundation of **tonotopy** (frequency-place mapping).
- **Drug diffusion** is governed by Fick's second law $\partial C/\partial t = D \nabla^2 C$, mathematically identical to the heat equation, solvable with FTCS or Crank–Nicolson.
- **Order of accuracy** must be quoted: $O(k) + O(h^2)$ for explicit, $O(k^2) + O(h^2)$ for Crank–Nicolson and central wave scheme.
- **Mesh ratio $r$** is the key stability parameter — always compute and report it.
- **Boundary conditions** (Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin) must be explicitly written and the discretization must accommodate them (e.g., ghost nodes for Neumann).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Computational Modelling of Biosystems

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Computational Modelling of Biosystems is the science of using mathematical formulations, numerical algorithms, and computer simulations to represent, analyse, and predict the behaviour of biological and physiological systems. In the KTU 2024 PEBRT744 syllabus, it encompasses the numerical treatment of bioelectric phenomena (ECG, EEG), endocrine feedback loops (glucose–insulin), and pharmacokinetic processes (renal clearance) using open repositories and open-source numerical toolkits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly clusters the topic into two pedagogical halves:
> **(a) Tool Layer** — PhysioNet, PhysioBank, PhysioToolkit, and Python neuronal packages (sources of data & software).
> **(b) Application Layer** — ECG, EEG, Glucose-Insulin Regulation, and Renal Clearance (mathematical models solved numerically).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — A Simple Real-World Analogy

Imagine a pilot learning to fly a Boeing 747. She does **not** learn by crashing real aircraft; she uses a **flight simulator** — a computer program that mathematically mimics the aerodynamic forces, fuel dynamics, and control responses of the plane. Computational modelling of biosystems is *exactly* this idea, applied to the human body:

- The **human body** = the Boeing 747 (too complex and too risky to experiment on directly).
- The **mathematical equations** (ODEs, PDEs, difference equations) = the aerodynamic laws.
- The **computer** = the simulator cockpit.
- The **PhysioNet databases** = the recorded black-box flight data of previous flights, used to validate the simulator.

By changing "knobs" in the simulator (e.g., "what if insulin secretion drops to 30 % of normal?"), biomedical engineers can predict *what would happen to the patient* — without ever touching a real patient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Computational Bioscience (Memorise for KTU):**
> 1. **Data Layer** — PhysioBank (repositories of real signals).
> 2. **Software Layer** — PhysioToolkit + Python libraries (the engines that crunch numbers).
> 3. **Model Layer** — Differential equations representing physiology (ECG dynamics, Bergman minimal model, Hodgkin–Huxley neuron, etc.).

## 1.3 PhysioNet, PhysioBank, PhysioToolkit — Core Definitions

| Component | Formal Definition (KTU Board Expectation) | One-Line Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **PhysioNet** | A free, web-accessible resource maintained by the *MIT Computational Physiology Laboratory* that hosts PhysioBank and PhysioToolkit, providing open access to physiologic signal archives and analytical software. | The "online library + laboratory" for biomedical signal research. |
| **PhysioBank** | A large, growing archive of well-characterized digital recordings of physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, GSR, respiration, etc.) and related time-series, with associated metadata, used by the research community. | The "library shelves" — actual patient data. |
| **PhysioToolkit** | A large, growing library of open-source software for signal processing, time-series analysis, linear algebra, statistics, nonlinear dynamics, and physiological modelling, written in **C, C++, Fortran, MATLAB, and Python**. | The "tools" — the analysers and modellers. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequently Tested Fact:** PhysioNet was founded in **1999** by **Dr. Ary L. Goldberger** of *Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center* and *Harvard Medical School*. It is hosted at **https://physionet.org/**. The trio PhysioNet + PhysioBank + PhysioToolkit is often referred to collectively as **"Physio* resources"**.

## 1.4 Python Packages for Simulating Neuronal Activity

Although the KTU 2024 regulation prohibits source-code dumps in the answer script, students **must** know the names, affiliations, and core use of these packages:

| Package | Affiliation / Origin | Core Use |
|---|---|---|
| **NEURON** | Yale University / Michael Hines | Detailed compartmental modelling of individual neurons with Hodgkin–Huxley type channels. |
| **Brian** | École Normale Supérieure, Paris | Spiking neural-network simulator written in Python. |
| **NEST** | NEST Initiative (Jülich, Germany) | Large-scale, point-neuron network simulation. |
| **PyNN** | Andrew Davison (UNIC, CNRS) | Simulator-independent neural-network description language. |
| **Brian2** | Stimberg, Goodman, Benichoux | Brian's modern, code-generation-based successor. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **NEURON** (a *neuron* simulator) with **Nest** (a *network* simulator). NEURON is best for **single-neuron biophysics**; NEST is best for **large networks of point neurons** (thousands to millions of cells).

## 1.5 Engineering Metrics & Standard Constants to Memorise

- **Renal filtration threshold:** GFR ≈ **125 mL/min** in a healthy adult.
- **Standard body fluid glucose:** **70–110 mg/dL** (fasting).
- **ECG standard sampling rate:** **500 Hz** (clinical); **360 Hz** (legacy MIT-BIH).
- **EEG standard sampling rate:** **160–512 Hz** depending on montage.
- **Goldberger's rule for QRS detection:** Pan–Tompkins algorithm operates at **200 Hz** internal rate.
- **Hodgkin–Huxley resting potential:** **−70 mV**.
- **Cockcroft–Gault constant:** **k = 1.04 (male)**, **0.85 (female)**.

> [!NOTE]
> All values above are **gold-standard clinical or simulation metrics** that the KTU board expects to appear in any model-based question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Biosystem Simulation

A KTU-board-style description of computational biosystem modelling follows a strict five-layer pipeline. Every model — whether ECG, EEG, glucose, or renal — passes through these layers:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — signals/measurements are pulled from PhysioBank (e.g., `mitdb/100` for ECG).
2. **Preprocessing Layer** — PhysioToolkit routines (`wfdb`, `rdsamp`, `ann2rr`) clean and segment data.
3. **Mathematical Model Layer** — ODEs / PDEs / difference equations are formulated.
4. **Numerical Solver Layer** — Forward Euler, RK4, Heun, or implicit stiff solvers (CVODE) advance time.
5. **Visualisation / Validation Layer** — output is compared against the original PhysioBank record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this layered view matters:** KTU questions on this module often begin with *"Briefly explain the workflow of computational modelling of a biosystem"* — students who simply list three items lose 2 marks; those who list **five explicit layers** with one PhysioBank example each score full.

## 2.2 ECG Simulation — McSharry–Clifford Dynamical Model

The McSharry–Clifford model (2003) represents a single cardiac cycle as a **3-D trajectory** in state-space that moves around a limit cycle.

### State Variables
- $x(t)$ — $x$-coordinate of the trajectory in state-space.
- $y(t)$ — $y$-coordinate.
- $z(t)$ — $z$-coordinate.

### Governing Equations

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha\,x - \omega\,y$$

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \alpha\,y + \omega\,x$$

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = -\sum_{i \in \{P,Q,R,S,T\}} a_i\,\Delta\theta_i\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta\theta_i^{2}}{2\,b_i^{2}}\right) - (z - z_{0})$$

where $\Delta\theta_i = (\theta - \theta_i)$ and $\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(y, x)$.

### Parameter Dictionary (high-yield for KTU)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\omega$ | Angular heart rate | $\tfrac{2\pi}{RR}$ | rad/s |
| $\alpha$ | Radial contraction coefficient | $1 - \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$ | s$^{-1}$ |
| $a_i$ | Amplitude of wave $i$ | P: 0.25, Q: 0.025, R: 1.6, S: 0.25, T: 0.35 | mV |
| $b_i$ | Width of wave $i$ | P: 0.25, Q: 0.1, R: 0.1, S: 0.1, T: 0.25 | rad |
| $\theta_i$ | Angular position | P: −π/3, Q: −π/12, R: 0, S: π/12, T: π/2 | rad |
| $z_0$ | Baseline offset | 0 (configurable) | mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This model is used in **pacemaker firmware testing**, **defibrillator algorithm validation**, and **ECG denoising benchmark** generation, because it can synthesise a clean reference ECG when patient data is too noisy.

## 2.3 EEG Simulation — Forward & Inverse Problems

EEG signals arise from cortical current dipoles. The numerical modelling is split into:

- **Forward Problem:** Given the *source* (dipole location & orientation), compute the *scalp potentials*. Solved using the **head model** (spherical three-shell, Boundary Element Method, or Finite Element Method).
- **Inverse Problem:** Given the *scalp measurements*, estimate the *source*. Ill-posed → solved with **sLORETA**, **MNE**, or **beamforming**.

### High-Yield Forward Formula (Concentric Three-Sphere Head Model)

For a radial dipole at radius $r_0$ in a 3-shell head (brain r$_1$, skull r$_2$, scalp r$_3$), the surface potential at angle $\theta$ is:

$$V(R, \theta) = \frac{I\,r_0^{2}\,\cos\theta}{4\pi\sigma_{3}\,R^{2}}\,\Big[\,2\,\tfrac{r_0\,R}{r_{2}^{2}} + \tfrac{(R - r_0)\,R}{R^{2}} + \dots \Big]$$

(Exact analytical form is long; students must remember that **conductivity ratios** $\sigma_1 : \sigma_2 : \sigma_3 = 1 : \tfrac{1}{80} : 1$ are used, with the **skull being 80× more resistive than soft tissue**.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently Asked:** *Why is skull conductivity so low?* Because bone has very low water content, so it conducts ionic current poorly. This is the physical reason EEG has **poor spatial resolution (~1–2 cm)** compared to MEG.

## 2.4 Glucose Regulation — Bergman Minimal Model

The **Bergman Minimal Model** (1981) describes glucose disappearance after an intravenous glucose tolerance test (IVGTT). It uses **three ODEs**:

$$\frac{dG}{dt} = -p_1\,(G - G_b) - X\,G + D(t)$$

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -p_2}\,X + p_3}\,(I - I_b)$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \gamma\,(G - h)^{+}\,t - n\,(I - I_b)$$

### Parameter Reference Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $G$ | Plasma glucose | 70–110 (fasting) | mg/dL |
| $I$ | Plasma insulin | 5–15 (fasting) | µU/mL |
| $X$ | Remote insulin action | 0 (basal) | min$^{-1}$ |
| $p_1$ | Glucose effectiveness | $0.03$ | min$^{-1}$ |
| $p_2$ | Insulin action decay | $0.02$ | min$^{-1}$ |
| $p_3$ | Insulin action gain | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | min$^{-2}$ per µU/mL |
| $\gamma$ | Pancreatic responsivity | variable | (µU/mL)/(min·mg/dL) |
| $n$ | Hepatic insulin clearance | $0.2$ | min$^{-1}$ |
| $h$ | Pancreatic threshold | $80$ | mg/dL |
| $G_b$, $I_b$ | Basal values | 90, 10 | mg/dL, µU/mL |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Bergman = B*l*ood Glucose & Insulin feedback G*I*X with three parameters **p₁, p₂, p₃**."* The subscript 1→G only, 2→X only, 3→X receives from I.

## 2.5 Diabetes & Insulin Regulation

- **Type 1 Diabetes** — autoimmune destruction of pancreatic $\beta$-cells. Model: **$p_3 \to 0$** (insulin action gain collapses).
- **Type 2 Diabetes** — insulin resistance. Model: **$p_1}$ reduced, $p_3$ reduced, $n$ increased**.
- **Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)** — extended model adds ketogenesis equation.

A common clinical target: **HbA1c < 7.0 %** corresponds to mean plasma glucose ≈ **154 mg/dL**.

## 2.6 Renal Clearance — Pharmacokinetic Modelling

**Renal Clearance ($C_x$)** is defined as the *virtual volume of plasma completely cleared of substance $x$ per unit time*. The fundamental governing equation is:

$$C_x = \frac{U_x \cdot V}{P_x}$$

where $U_x$ = urine concentration, $V$ = urine flow rate, $P_x$ = plasma concentration.

### Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) Estimation

The **Cockcroft–Gault equation** (for creatinine clearance, in mL/min):

$$C_{Cr} = \frac{(140 - \text{Age}) \cdot W}{72 \cdot S_{Cr}} \cdot k$$

where $k = 1.0$ for males, $0.85$ for females; $W$ = body weight (kg); $S_{Cr}$ = serum creatinine (mg/dL).

The **MDRD** (Modification of Diet in Renal Disease) equation is an alternative:

$$\text{GFR} = 175 \cdot (S_{Cr})^{-1.154} \cdot (\text{Age})^{-0.203} \cdot 0.742^{\text{Female}} \cdot 1.212^{\text{Black}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world use:** $C_{Cr}$ estimates the **excretion rate of drugs** such as gentamicin, vancomycin, and metformin. Engineers use these formulas in **closed-loop artificial pancreas** design to titrate insulin safely.

## 2.7 Numerical Solver Selection — KTU Decision Table

| Equation Type | Recommended Solver | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Hodgkin–Huxley (stiff ODE) | **Backward Euler / CVODE** | $\tau \in [10^{-3}, 10^3]$ ms — extreme stiffness. |
| McSharry ECG (3-D) | **RK4** | Mild stiffness, needs accuracy for P/QRS/T peaks. |
| Bergman glucose | **RK4** | Non-stiff, slow timescales (minutes). |
| Renal clearance | **Forward Euler** | Explicit algebraic update, low dynamics. |

## 2.8 Why All This Matters in Engineering

- **Pacemaker designers** use McSharry ECG simulation to test arrhythmia-detection firmware.
- **Closed-loop insulin pumps** use Bergman model as their inner simulation core.
- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** engineers use EEG forward solvers to decide electrode placement.
- **Dialysis machine manufacturers** use clearance equations to size membrane surface area.
- **Neural prosthetics** use NEURON / NEST to design stimulation protocols that avoid runaway excitation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: McSharry–Clifford ECG as a Limit-Cycle Trajectory

The classical derivation starts from the requirement that **a synthetic ECG must reproduce the P-Q-R-S-T morphology**.

### Step 1 — Define the State Vector

Let $\mathbf{s}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))^{T}$ be a 3-D state vector. We demand that the projection of $\mathbf{s}(t)$ onto the $z$-axis, when plotted against time, looks like the P-Q-R-S-T complex.

### Step 2 — Impose a Rotating Circular Trajectory in the $xy$-Plane

For a healthy heart beating at $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{RR}$ rad/s, the $xy$ motion is a **circular limit cycle**. The radius $r$ should be close to **1** for unit-normalised output.

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = -\omega\,y + \alpha\,x$$

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \omega\,x + \alpha\,y$$

where $\alpha = 1 - \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$ is a radial restoring term that keeps the trajectory on the unit circle.

### Step 3 — Couple the $z$ Motion to the Angular Position

The angular position in the $xy$-plane is:

$$\theta(t) = \mathrm{atan2}(y(t), x(t))$$

A single depolarisation wave is a **Gaussian bump** in $\theta$. The $z$-velocity is then the sum of five such Gaussians (one per P, Q, R, S, T wave):

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = -\sum_{i} a_i \cdot \Delta\theta_i \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta\theta_i^{2}}{2\,b_i^{2}}\right) - (z - z_0)$$

where $\Delta\theta_i = (\theta - \theta_i)\,\mathrm{mod}\,2\pi$, centred at angular position $\theta_i$.

### Step 4 — Interpret the Output Signal

The **ECG signal** is taken as the $z$-component:

$$\text{ECG}(t) = z(t)$$

### Step 5 — Tune the Parameters for Realism

For a heart rate of **60 bpm**, $RR = 1$ s, so $\omega = 2\pi$ rad/s. The default parameters in the table of Section 2.2 yield a clinically realistic morphology. The **R-peak amplitude** is set by $a_R = 1.6$ (largest), and the **T-wave** is broader (larger $b_T = 0.25$) — exactly matching clinical ECG.

### Step 6 — Why the $-(z - z_0)$ Term?

This is a **leakage / decay** term that brings $z$ back to baseline $z_0$ after each Gaussian contribution, mimicking **repolarisation**. Without it, the baseline would drift upwards over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget the **modulo-2π** operation on $\Delta\theta_i$. Without it, the bumps only appear once per cycle. The `mod 2π` is what allows the model to fire repeatedly as $\theta$ keeps circulating.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bergman Minimal Model (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Physiological Justification

After an IVGTT, glucose ($G$) rises sharply, then falls. Insulin ($I$) follows with a delay, then decays. The remote insulin compartment ($X$) is an *abstract* tissue-level insulin action that decays slowly.

### Step 2 — Glucose Equation

Glucose falls by two mechanisms:
1. **Glucose effectiveness** (insulin-independent) — proportional to $G - G_b$.
2. **Insulin-dependent uptake** — proportional to $X \cdot G$.

External input $D(t)$ is the IVGTT bolus (modelled as a Dirac pulse at $t=0$).

$$\frac{dG}{dt} = -p_1\,(G - G_b) - X\,G + D(t)$$

### Step 3 — Remote Insulin Equation

$X$ is generated by plasma insulin (with delay constant $p_3$) and decays with rate $p_2$:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -p_2\,X + p_3\,(I - I_b)$$

### Step 4 — Insulin Equation

The pancreas secretes insulin only when glucose **exceeds the threshold** $h$. The $(G - h)^{+}$ term is the Heaviside / ReLU operator.

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \gamma\,(G - h)^{+}\,t - n\,(I - I_b)$$

### Step 5 — Solve Numerically

Apply RK4 with step $h = 1$ min over a 180-min IVGTT window. Initial conditions: $G(0) = G_0$ (≈ 250 mg/dL after bolus), $X(0) = 0$, $I(0) = I_b$.

### Step 6 — Extract Clinical Indices

- **Insulin Sensitivity Index $S_I = p_3 / p_2$** (units: $10^{-4}$ min$^{-1}$ per µU/mL).
- **Glucose Effectiveness $S_G = p_1$**.
- **Acute Insulin Response $AIR_g = \int_0^{10}(I - I_b)\,dt$**.

These three indices classify patients into **Normal, IGT (Impaired Glucose Tolerance), and Type 2 Diabetic** categories.

## 3.3 Derivation: Renal Clearance from Mass Balance

### Step 1 — Define the Concept

If $V$ is the urine flow rate (mL/min) and $U_x$ is the urine concentration of substance $x$, the **excretion rate** is $E_x = U_x \cdot V$ (mg/min).

### Step 2 — Definition of Clearance

Clearance is the **virtual plasma volume** that would contain the same mass of $x$ that is excreted per minute. Equating $E_x$ to $C_x \cdot P_x$:

$$C_x \cdot P_x = U_x \cdot V$$

$$\implies C_x = \frac{U_x \cdot V}{P_x}$$

### Step 3 — Application: Creatinine Clearance

Creatinine is filtered freely by the glomerulus and **not reabsorbed**, so its clearance approximates GFR:

$$C_{Cr} = \frac{U_{Cr} \cdot V}{P_{Cr}}$$

### Step 4 — Cockcroft–Gault Empirical Refinement

Empirically, $C_{Cr}$ decreases with age (declining nephron mass) and is proportional to lean body weight. The empirical fit gives:

$$C_{Cr} = \frac{(140 - \text{Age}) \cdot W}{72 \cdot S_{Cr}} \cdot k$$

where $k$ accounts for lower muscle mass in females (less creatinine generation).

## 3.4 Derivation: Hodgkin–Huxley Neuron (4-ODE System) — Conceptual

The membrane potential $V_m$ obeys:

$$C_m\,\frac{dV_m}{dt} = I_{ext} - g_{Na}\,m^{3}\,h\,(V_m - E_{Na}) - g_K\,n^{4}\,(V_m - E_K) - g_L\,(V_m - E_L)$$

Gate dynamics:

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m(1 - m) - \beta_m\,m$$

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \alpha_h(1 - h) - \beta_h\,h$$

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \alpha_n(1 - n) - \beta_n\,n$$

with $\alpha, \beta$ as sigmoidal functions of $V_m$. Standard values: $C_m = 1\,\mu\text{F/cm}^{2}$, $g_{Na} = 120$, $g_K = 36$, $g_L = 0.3$ (all in mS/cm²), $E_{Na} = +50$ mV, $E_K = -77$ mV, $E_L = -54$ mV, $V_{rest} = -70$ mV.

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU PEBRT744 syllabus:** Students must know the *form* of the HH equations and the typical parameter values, but are not expected to derive the $\alpha, \beta$ sigmoid expressions.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Bergman Model Step

> **Question (typical KTU Part A):** A normal adult undergoes IVGTT. At $t = 0$, $G = 250$ mg/dL, $X = 0$, $I = I_b = 10$ µU/mL. Using $p_1 = 0.03$ min$^{-1}$, $p_2 = 0.02$ min$^{-1}$, $p_3 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$, $G_b = 90$, and RK4 with $h = 1$ min, compute $G(1)$ and $I(1)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $D(t)$:** $D(0) = D_{bolus} \approx 300$ mg/dL·min spike; for $t \geq 1$, $D = 0$.

**Step 2 — Derivatives at $t=0$:**

$$\frac{dG}{dt} = -0.03 \cdot (250 - 90) - 0 \cdot 250 + 0 = -4.8 \text{ mg/dL/min}$$

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -0.02 \cdot 0 + 5\times 10^{-5} \cdot (10 - 10) = 0$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \gamma\,(250 - 80)^{+}\,0 - 0.2\,(10 - 10) = 0$$

**Step 3 — RK4 advances** (full computation omitted for brevity, but the final estimates after 1 minute are):
- $G(1) \approx 245$ mg/dL (small drop from insulin-independent effectiveness).
- $X(1) \approx 0$ (no insulin action yet, $I$ has not risen above $I_b$).
- $I(1) \approx 10$ µU/mL (unchanged, pancreas not yet stimulated).

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Insulin secretion only begins after $G > h = 80$ and after the **time-multiplier** $t$ accumulates — this delay produces the realistic slow insulin rise observed clinically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark each for derivative evaluation, 1 mark for RK4 step, 1 mark for interpretation. Total = 3 marks — perfect for KTU Part A.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow — Computational Biosystem Modelling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PhysioBank Database] --> B[wfdb rdsamp]
    B --> C[Raw Physiological Signal]
    C --> D[PhysioToolkit Preprocessing]
    D --> E[Filtered and Segmented Data]
    E --> F[Mathematical Model Formulation]
    F --> G{ODE or PDE?}
    G -->|ODE| H[RK4 / Forward Euler]
    G -->|PDE| I[Finite Difference or FEM]
    H --> J[Synthetic Time Series]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Validation against PhysioBank]
    K --> L{Within Tolerance?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Deploy Model to Clinical or Engineering Application]
    L -->|No| N[Parameter Re-tuning]
    N --> F
```

## 4.2 ECG Simulation Architecture (McSharry Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heart Rate RR] --> B[Angular Velocity omega]
    B --> C[3D Limit Cycle xy Plane]
    C --> D[Angular Position theta]
    D --> E[5 Gaussian Bumps P Q R S T]
    E --> F[dz/dt Summation]
    F --> G[Synthetic ECG z t]
    G --> H[Visualisation and PQRST Peak Detection]
```

## 4.3 Glucose-Insulin Feedback Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plasma Glucose G] -->|Stimulus| B[Pancreatic beta cells]
    B -->|Secretion| C[Plasma Insulin I]
    C -->|Remote compartment| D[Insulin Action X]
    D -->|Uptake| A
    A -->|Threshold h| E[Heaviside Gate]
    E -->|Modulation| C
    A -->|Glucose Effectiveness p1| F[Insulin-Independent Uptake]
    F --> A
```

## 4.4 EEG Forward & Inverse Modelling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cortical Dipole Source] --> B[Head Model 3-shell or BEM]
    B --> C[Scalp Potential Map]
    C --> D[EEG Electrodes]
    D --> E[Inverse Solver sLORETA or MNE]
    E --> F[Reconstructed Source Map]
    A -. true location .-> G[Validation]
    F -. estimated location .-> G
```

## 4.5 Renal Clearance Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plasma Concentration Px] --> B{Renal Pathway}
    B -->|Filtered| C[GFR * Px]
    B -->|Secreted| D[Secretion Rate]
    B -->|Reabsorbed| E[Reabsorption Rate]
    C --> F[Excretion Ux * V]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Clearance Cx = UxV / Px]
    G --> H[Compare to GFR]
    H -->|Cx < GFR| I[Net Reabsorption]
    H -->|Cx = GFR| J[Net Zero Secretion]
    H -->|Cx > GFR| K[Net Secretion]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define PhysioNet, PhysioBank and PhysioToolkit. State one clinical use of each in computational biomedical research. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **PhysioNet** is a free online platform hosted by the MIT Computational Physiology Laboratory that provides access to physiologic signal archives and analysis software. *Clinical use:* It is the standard source for downloading **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** for benchmarking ECG R-peak detectors.
- **PhysioBank** is a large archive of well-characterised digital recordings of physiological signals such as ECG, EEG and EMG, with associated metadata. *Clinical use:* Provides **MIMIC-III** critical-care records used in sepsis prediction models.
- **PhysioToolkit** is an open-source library of signal-processing and physiological-modelling routines. *Clinical use:* Provides the `wfdb` Python library used to read PhysioBank records in clinical ML pipelines.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest]**
> **Q2.** With a neat block diagram, explain the workflow of computational modelling of a bioengineering system. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 points, 1 mark each):**
1. **Data Acquisition:** Real physiological signals are obtained from PhysioBank (e.g., `mitdb/100` for ECG).
2. **Model Formulation:** A set of mathematical equations (e.g., McSharry for ECG, Bergman for glucose) is proposed to mimic the system.
3. **Numerical Simulation:** ODEs/PDEs are solved using RK4 or Euler method on a digital computer.
4. **Validation:** The simulated output is compared against the original PhysioBank record; if the error exceeds tolerance, parameters are retuned. *(3 layers yield 3 marks; full 5-layer answer scores 5.)*

---

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

2. **Type 1** — autoimmune $\beta$-cell destruction; in Bergman terms $p_3 \to 0$ (no insulin action gain). **(2 marks)**
3. **Type 2** — insulin resistance; reduced $p_1$ and $p_3$, increased $n$. **(2 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

2. **Brian2** — modern Python simulator with code generation, written in pure Python, developed at ENS Paris. **(1.75 marks)**
3. **NEST** — point-neuron network simulator for large-scale networks, supports SpiNNaker neuromorphic hardware. **(1.75 marks)**
4. **PyNN** — simulator-independent description language, code generation for both NEURON and NEST. **(1.75 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

2. Software Layer — PhysioToolkit `wfdb` for reading. **(1.5 marks)**
3. Model Layer — ODEs (McSharry, Bergman). **(1.5 marks)**
4. Solver Layer — RK4 / Euler integration. **(1.5 marks)**
5. Validation Layer — comparison with PhysioBank ground truth. **(1 mark)**

> **Q6 (b) — 7 Marks.** Compare the numerical properties of the McSharry ECG, Bergman glucose, and Hodgkin–Huxley neuron models. Which solver is appropriate for each and why?

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**
1. McSharry — 3-D, mildly stiff, RK4. **(2 marks)**
2. Bergman — 3-D, non-stiff, slow timescale, RK4 with $h = 1$ min. **(2 marks)**
3. Hodgkin–Huxley — 4-D, extremely stiff (ion channel gating τ varies over 4 orders of magnitude), needs **Backward Euler or CVODE**. **(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callouts (Lose 1–2 marks if missed):**
> 1. **Do not confuse NEST with NEURON.** NEST = network, NEURON = single-neuron biophysics. Marks lost in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **Always state units** (mg/dL, µU/mL, mL/min, mS/cm²) — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Cockcroft–Gault constant $k$** — forgetting to multiply by 0.85 for females leads to 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **McSharry $\theta_i$ values** — P-wave is at $-\pi/3$ (not $+\pi/3$); R-wave is at $0$ (not at $2\pi$). Forgetting the `mod 2π` is the most common error.
> 5. **Bergman $(G - h)^{+}$** — this is a ReLU/Heaviside, *not* a smooth function. Forgetting the Heaviside step costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skull conductivity ratio** — $1 : 1/80 : 1$ is for **brain : skull : scalp**, *not* the other way. Reverse the order and lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Always cross-validate** simulated output against a PhysioBank record — a model-only answer without validation is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **PhysioNet** = online platform; **PhysioBank** = data archive; **PhysioToolkit** = software library.
- [ ] PhysioNet was founded in **1999** by **Dr. Ary L. Goldberger** at **Harvard / Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center**.
- [ ] **Python neuronal packages:** **NEURON** (single-cell), **Brian2** (spiking net, Pythonic), **NEST** (large network, SpiNNaker), **PyNN** (simulator-independent).
- [ ] **McSharry–Clifford ECG** is a 3-D limit-cycle model with 5 Gaussian bumps (P, Q, R, S, T) and a $-(z - z_0)$ baseline restoration.
- [ ] **R-peak is the largest** ($a_R = 1.6$), **T-wave is the broadest** ($b_T = 0.25$).
- [ ] **EEG skull conductivity is 1/80 of soft tissue** — reason for low spatial resolution.
- [ ] **Forward problem** = source → scalp; **Inverse problem** = scalp → source (ill-posed, uses sLORETA/MNE).
- [ ] **Bergman model** = 3 ODEs ($G, X, I$); 3 indices ($S_I, S_G, AIR_g$).
- [ ] **Type 1 DM** corresponds to $p_3 \to 0$; **Type 2 DM** corresponds to reduced $p_1, p_3$ and increased $n$.
- [ ] **Heaviside gate** $(G - h)^{+}$ is essential to Bergman insulin secretion — *never smooth it*.
- [ ] **Cockcroft–Gault:** $C_{Cr} = (140 - A)W / (72 \cdot S_{Cr}) \cdot k$; $k = 1$ male, $0.85$ female.
- [ ] **Renal clearance fundamental equation:** $C_x = U_x V / P_x$.
- [ ] **Stiff ODEs** (Hodgkin–Huxley) require implicit solvers (Backward Euler, CVODE); **non-stiff** (Bergman, McSharry) can use RK4.
- [ ] **HbA1c target** for diabetic patients: **< 7.0 %** (≈ mean glucose 154 mg/dL).
- [ ] **Standard GFR:** **125 mL/min**; standard serum creatinine: **0.7–1.3 mg/dL**.
- [ ] **ECG sampling rate (clinical):** 500 Hz; (MIT-BIH legacy): 360 Hz.
- [ ] **Hodgkin–Huxley resting potential:** −70 mV; **$E_{Na}$** = +50 mV; **$E_K$** = −77 mV.
- [ ] **Five-layer computational pipeline:** Data → Software → Model → Solver → Validation. Always draw the block diagram in 7-mark answers.
- [ ] **EEG inverse problem** is ill-posed → requires **regularisation** (e.g., Tikhonov).
- [ ] **BCI, epilepsy focus localisation, dialysis machine design, artificial pancreas** = four "industry-relevance" hooks examiners love.
- [ ] **Reference frame** in 7-mark answers: always start with a definition (1 mark), then equations (2 marks), then numerical example (2 marks), then application (2 marks).
